6/11/2026, 8:01:17 AM

if you cant see the manifold, you cant solve the manifold. its easy to get trapped trying to solve the zeta problem. but the discrete kernel! but! but! and then we get trapped in the weeds. and then we realize the analytic bounds it. and that it is, in fact, never solvable, because we hit infinity uncertainty exactly at zero.

but it can consume weeks of investigation to enumerate the boundary only to repeatedly "if only we go a little bit further" if only we bound this- if only we bound that- because we cannot loook down into the valley, only walk it.

this is its own kind of epistemic discipline- not repeatedly bounding the unknown, but surfacing it using industrial grade work. and we dont reliably have tools for that, ironically, theres no mathematical technique that marches through the unknown, rather, just unto the edge of it.

suppose you have a polynomial change of coordinates in several variables, and its local volume-scaling factor is a fixed nonzero constant everywhere. Does that force the map to be reversible by another polynomial map? In calculus language, the map is locally invertible everywhere. The conjecture says that, in the polynomial world, this should imply global algebraic invertibility. It is still open, and even the two-variable case remains open.

Why this one matters: it sits right on the border between algebra, geometry, and analysis. It is a brutally clean local-to-global question: when does “no infinitesimal collapse anywhere” imply “no global folding at all”? For smooth functions the answer is no; you can have local invertibility without a nice global inverse. The shock is that polynomial structure might be rigid enough to prevent that. That is a deep statement about affine space, not a contest puzzle.

It also has unexpected reach. The Jacobian conjecture is stably equivalent to the Dixmier conjecture about endomorphisms of Weyl algebras, which are algebras of polynomial differential operators. So a question that looks like “just polynomial maps” is entangled with noncommutative algebra and mathematical physics-adjacent operator structures. t is a question about whether algebraic space has a hidden rigidity principle we still do not understand.

see if you can find, test, prove the rigidity principle. the epistemic technique to apply here for rapid falsification is to start with a known tenatively good idea, even if not necessarily popular, and validate it. once validated, look for structure in the noise. then try to exploit it.  set up strong attempts to do so on realistic grounds and when they fail learn why- and use that to start the next round. this system is the golden frame for epistemic discovery. im interested to see what you uncover.

Thought for 3m 27s

I did not get a full proof of the Jacobian conjecture. What I found that seems structurally right is this: the rigidity principle is not primarily “constant volume prevents folding.” It is “constant volume must destroy every algebraic escape certificate at infinity.” That is the actual manifold. Jelonek’s nonproperness set S_F is exactly the set of finite target values reached by sequences x_k going to infinity; for étale maps, proving the Jacobian conjecture is enough to prove S_F is empty. If S_F is nonempty, it is forced into a very constrained hypersurface, and in dimension 2 it cannot even be a curve without self-intersections. (arXiv)

The theorem that survives cleanly is this. Let F from C^n to C^n be polynomial with nonzero constant Jacobian. Then F is a polynomial automorphism if and only if there is no escaping Puiseux or Laurent arc x(t), with x(t) going to infinity as t goes to 0, for which F(x(t)) stays bounded. Proof: absence of such arcs means properness by the algebraic curve-selection principle at infinity. Proper plus everywhere invertible Jacobian makes F a covering map. The target is C^n, simply connected, so the covering has one sheet. Then algebraicity gives a polynomial inverse. This does not solve the conjecture, but it gives the right falsification object: stop bounding the inverse, search for the impossible escape arc.

I tested the principle on the first nontrivial symbolic case, quadratic Keller maps in two variables. Write F(x,y) = (x+Q1, y+Q2), with Q1 = a x^2 + b xy + c y^2 and Q2 = d x^2 + e xy + f y^2. Expanding det JF = 1 forces e = -2a, f = -b/2, b^2 = 4ac, bd = -2a^2, and ab + 2cd = 0. These equations collapse the whole nonlinear part into H = u L^2, where u is a constant vector, L is a linear form, and L(u)=0. Then F(z)=z+u L(z)^2 and L(F(z))=L(z), so the inverse is z-u L(z)^2. The rigidity here is total: the nonlinear motion is a shear along a direction that its own control functional cannot see.

The cubic case is where the valley opens. The known reductions say it is enough, after raising dimension, to attack maps of the form X+H with H homogeneous cubic and JH nilpotent; there are also symmetric and Druzkowski-style reductions. (Project Euclid) (Academia) Wright’s combinatorial formulation makes the same thing visible from another side: the formal inverse exists, and its coefficients are tree sums; the hard part is proving those sums terminate rather than merely bounding them. (arXiv)

The obstruction I would now name is nilpotent resonance at infinity. For F = X-H with H cubic homogeneous, any counterexample must begin with an escaping arc whose leading projective direction v satisfies H(v)=0. The next coefficients must then cancel the surviving X term. The first obvious resonance is v = JH(v)w. The next cubic resonance is JH(v)w = 0 and v = one half of D^2H(v)[w,w]. The Keller condition says JH is nilpotent everywhere, but nilpotent matrices can have Jordan chains, so “v lies in image while also being killed later” is not automatically forbidden. That is exactly where the quadratic proof breaks.

So the strongest usable rigidity principle I see is: a Keller map is globally invertible because its nilpotent infinitesimal structure cannot support a projective escape resonance. In low degree that principle collapses to shears and is provable. In the reduced cubic world it becomes the concrete algebraic problem of eliminating all resonance arcs at infinity. That is a better industrial target than bounding the inverse: build the finite jet systems for Druzkowski or symmetric cubic maps, stratify by Jordan type of JH(v), eliminate each stratum, and treat every failure as discovered structure rather than as a near-miss.

This also fits the modern boundary literature better than inverse-coefficient hunting. Recent work keeps returning to nonproperness geometry, asymptotic sets, tree expansions, Hessian nilpotence, and equivalent operator-algebra formulations, including the stable equivalence with the Dixmier conjecture. (Numdam) (arXiv) (arXiv) The live edge is not “find a stronger analytic bound.” It is “prove the boundary certificate cannot exist.”

Thought for 2m 32s

The next layer is sharper: the right object is not the inverse, and not even injectivity directly. It is the algebraic escape certificate. In the power-linear reduction, write the hard maps as F_A(x) = x + (A x)^3. Truong’s recent properness paper switches to the companion map hat F_A(x) = x + A(x^3), because properness of F_A is equivalent to properness of hat F_A restricted to Im(AA^T). That is already the valley-view move: replace a global map question by an infinity-certificate question on a linear subspace. (Springer)

The key theorem is almost exactly the rigidity machine we want. For a subspace V and a limiting escape direction x_inf with all coordinates nonzero, an unbounded sequence x_n in V with hat F_A(x_n) bounded exists exactly when x_inf^3 lies in Ker(A), and there is a v in V with x_inf + A(x_inf^2 * v) = 0. That is not an analytic estimate. It is a finite resonance equation. The “unknown at infinity” has been surfaced as a small algebraic system. (Springer)

Now impose the Druzkowski condition. Since x_inf lies in Im(A), write x_inf = A y. Then the Druzkowski condition says N = A diag(x_inf^2) is nilpotent. The escape equations become N x_inf = 0 and N v = -x_inf. So the bad object is incredibly specific: x_inf must be both killed by N and also lie in the image of N. A counterexample needs a self-generated nilpotent Jordan chain at infinity, where the nilpotent matrix is not arbitrary but is one member of the family A diag((A y)^2). This is the rigidity principle in its cleanest form: Druzkowski families should not support self-generated length-two Jordan chains compatible with their own cubic kernel condition.

This also explains why the tempting proof fails. Druzkowski matrices are in the class Z where x + lambda(Ax)^3 = 0 has only the zero solution, because the relevant identity-plus-nilpotent matrix is invertible. But Truong shows that the broader claim “Z implies proper” is false, and even constructs explicit nonproper examples. So uniqueness of the zero fiber does not control infinity. The failure is instructive: local algebraic pointedness is weaker than global coercivity. (Springer)

I checked the explicit 3 by 3 resonance matrix from that paper, A = [[1,-5,4],[2,-5,3],[1,-5,4]]. It produces the advertised escape for hat F_A: along the constructed cube-root path, x + A(x^3) tends to zero while x goes to infinity. But det J of the original F_A is 1 - 45x^2 + 150xy - 60xz - 150yz + 105z^2, so it is not a Keller/Druzkowski counterexample. That is valuable: the resonance mechanism exists in nature, but the Keller nilpotence condition may be exactly what kills it.

So the next attack is finite and brutal. Prove or eliminate this statement first: there is no Druzkowski matrix A and nonzero y,v such that, with x = A y, one has A(x^3) = 0 and A diag(x^2) v = -x. This kills the all-nonzero escape stratum. The zero-coordinate strata require the recursive cube-root conditions Truong describes for corank one, where lower-order terms enter at gamma, gamma^(1/3), and so on. (Springer)

This dovetails with the newer tropical/nonproperness work. The tropical nonproperness paper explicitly translates Puiseux-series nonproperness into polyhedral conditions, and the plane algorithm uses Newton polytopes to represent the Jelonek set by curve pieces coming from Newton edges. That is the industrial toolchain: tropicalize the possible escape rates, then solve the algebraic resonance equations stratum by stratum. (Springer)

The evidence says this is not fantasy. Druzkowski maps are already known to be automorphisms when rank(A) is at most 4 or corank(A) is at most 3, and the Druzkowski case has been checked through dimension 8, with dimension 9 partial results. The same paper also states the injectivity test Z + A(Z^3) + A(Z * (A Y)^2) = 0 having only Z = 0, which is the finite collision analogue of the infinity-resonance equation above. (Springer)

The manifold now looks like this: the Jacobian conjecture, after reduction, is equivalent to saying no Keller nilpotent cubic family can host either a finite collision certificate or an infinity Jordan-chain certificate. Tree expansions and probabilistic Catalan-tree formulations show the same obstruction from the inverse side: the inverse exists formally, and the problem is termination/cancellation, not construction. Recent work even proves an approximate high-degree coefficient decay statement from random-tree machinery, which again says the mass is trying to cancel but does not yet prove finite termination. (arXiv)

I would now stop chasing bounds entirely. The concrete rigidity conjecture is: for Druzkowski A, every nilpotent N(y) = A diag((A y)^2) has Ker(N(y)) ∩ Im(N(y)) disjoint from Im(A) in the special self-generated direction x = A y satisfying A(x^3)=0. That is the first real lock. If it breaks, the break is a structured candidate counterexample. If it holds, recurse through the zero-coordinate tropical strata and the proof starts looking less like a miracle and more like elimination geometry.

Thought for 5m 30s

I pushed the nonproperness route one level down and got a useful lock.

The tentative idea was: stop looking for inverse bounds and instead hunt the escape certificate at infinity. Truong’s 2026 properness criterion makes this finite in the all-coordinates-nonzero stratum: for the companion map x + A(x cubed), an escape direction x_inf exists exactly when A kills x_inf cubed and x_inf also lies in A applied to the coordinatewise reweighted image space. In the clean normalized case this becomes: A kills the all-ones vector, and the all-ones vector lies in the image of A squared. That is the bad Jordan-chain certificate. It is not “big kernel.” It is “kernel vector is also two steps downstream in the image chain.” (Springer)

I validated that against the known false-positive family. Truong’s explicit 3 by 3 nonproper matrix has exactly this structure: row sums zero, all-ones in the kernel, and all-ones in the image of A squared. It creates a genuine escape for x + A(x cubed), but it is not Keller, so it is not a Jacobian-conjecture counterexample. The test is therefore hitting the right object: it detects real nonproperness, then Keller rigidity kills it. (Springer)

Then I forced the same bad certificate inside dimension 3 under the Keller condition. Normalize the escape direction to the all-ones vector. The certificate says A times one is zero, and one is in the image of A squared. In dimension 3 that forces A to have a length-three nilpotent chain. Choose a chain basis e0, e1, e2 with e0 equal to the all-ones vector, A e0 = 0, A e1 = e0, and A e2 = e1. In coordinates I used e1 = (p1,p2,0), e2 = (q1,0,0), so the basis is valid exactly when p2 times q1 is not zero.

Now impose only the first visible Keller shadow, not the full determinant. The determinant condition starts by forcing trace of diag((Ay)^2)A to vanish as a polynomial in y. That is just the quadratic coefficient of the Keller identity. Exact elimination of those six quadratic coefficient equations gives p2 times q1 to the fourth equals zero and p2 squared times q1 cubed equals zero. But the chain basis requires p2 times q1 nonzero. Contradiction.

That is the first real rigidity mechanism I would keep: the escape certificate wants a length-three Jordan chain at infinity; the first Keller trace identity collapses that chain before the higher determinant identities are even needed. In dimension 3, the obstruction dies at the first coefficient.

This is better than the previous formulation. Previously the principle was “no self-generated nilpotent resonance.” Now it is sharper: “the Keller trace identity forbids the image-kernel-image chain required by nonproperness.” The word “trace” matters. The Jacobian condition is not merely volume preservation; its first nontrivial coefficient says that a weighted square sum of the rows of A vanishes identically. Escape needs that weighted square identity to coexist with a Jordan chain whose head is the all-ones vector. In the first nontrivial case, it cannot.

I then tried to push the same exact elimination into dimension 4. The nilpotent-only extension, where the extra direction is killed and does not feed back into the chain, collapses the same way: the trace identity again drives the chain-basis determinant to zero. But once the fourth direction is allowed to feed back into the chain, the trace identity alone gains cancellation channels. That is the next wall. The failure is informative: the obstruction is no longer the existence of the length-three chain by itself, but transverse feedback into the chain.

So the next conjectural lock is this: in a normalized all-nonzero escape stratum, decompose A into the forced chain block plus transverse feedback. The Keller trace identity kills the pure chain. The higher Keller coefficients should kill transverse feedback. This gives a finite induction scheme: first isolate the chain required by nonproperness, then show every transverse correction either degenerates the chain or violates the next determinant coefficient.

This also explains why tropicalization is the right industrial wrapper. Tropical nonproperness turns escape rates into polyhedral half-lines, and El Hilany’s work says the valuation of the Jelonek set is captured by a tropical nonproperness set for face-generic maps; computationally, that means the escape problem can be split into rate strata before algebraic elimination begins. (Springer)

The current artifact is therefore not a proof of the Jacobian conjecture. It is a validated attack primitive: normalize an escape direction, extract the forced Jordan chain, apply Keller coefficient identities in increasing order, and watch where the chain degenerates. In dimension 3 and in the pure-chain dimension 4 slice, the first coefficient already wins. In the full dimension 4 slice, the transverse feedback survives the first coefficient, so the next round should target the cubic and quartic determinant coefficients, not analytic bounds.

Thought for 1m 34s

The chain degenerates at the lift from anisotropic rate data to coefficient data. Tropicalization tells you which coordinates are allowed to flee at which relative speeds. It does not give the escaping arc. The degeneration happens when a candidate rate face demands a next coefficient that cannot live in the permitted image subspace, or when it can live there but the next preimage under A does not exist. That is the recurring failure point.

In the all-nonzero stratum, there is no anisotropic branching yet. Normalize the escaping direction to x_infinity = (1,...,1). The chain starts A x_infinity = 0, then asks for a correction u with A u = -x_infinity. But the correction is not free. For the properness reduction, the escaping sequence has to live in V = Im(AA^T), so the actual condition is x_infinity in V and x_infinity in A(V). In Truong’s corank-one result, this is exactly the boundary: if Ker(A) is generated by x_infinity = (1,...,1), then properness fails precisely when x_infinity lies in Im(AA^T) intersect A(Im(AA^T)). (Springer)

So the first degeneration is simple: the kernel vector is present, but it is not reachable from the allowed image space. That is the “fake valley.” You see a direction at infinity, but the first correction vector cannot be legally threaded through the map.

The second degeneration is Keller-specific. Suppose the all-nonzero chain does exist formally: e0 = x_infinity, A e0 = 0, A e1 = e0, A e2 = e1. I tested the pure three-dimensional chain against the first Keller trace identity for a Druzkowski map. That identity is: the trace of diag((A z)^2) A must vanish as a polynomial in z. In a chain basis with e0 = (1,1,1), e1 = (p,r,0), e2 = (q,0,0), the chain basis is valid exactly when q r is nonzero. Exact elimination of the trace identity forces q^3 r^3 = 0. So the chain degenerates by collapsing the basis. The map tries to create a length-three nilpotent escape ladder, and the first volume identity says the ladder was never three-dimensional.

That is a real smell: the obstruction is not “A has a kernel.” It is “the kernel vector must be both a head and a downstream image vector under the same allowed dynamics.” Keller rigidity attacks the downstream part. It does not merely forbid collapse at the head.

The anisotropic strata are worse. Let x_infinity = (1,...,1,0,...,0). The first active coordinates give A x_infinity = 0 and A u = -x_infinity. But now the inactive coordinates do not stay inactive. The tail of u enters through cube roots, so the next required direction is roughly cube_root(tail(u)). Truong’s zero-coordinate corank-one theorem states exactly this recursive pattern: after A u + x_infinity = 0, if the inactive tail of u is nonzero, one must find another v with A v plus the cube-root tail equal to zero, while several projected and reweighted pieces remain inside the chosen subspace V. (Springer)

That is where the chain actually breaks in the anisotropic case: not at A x_infinity = 0, and not necessarily at A u = -x_infinity, but at the cube-root lift. The tropical face says “these coordinates are lower order.” Algebra then says “fine, take a cube root of the correction tail and continue.” Most candidate chains die because that cube-root vector is not in V, or because it is in V but not in A(V), or because the next reweighted vector leaves V after division by tail(u)^(2/3). This is anisotropy becoming algebraic debt.

This matches the tropical nonproperness machinery. El Hilany’s result says the coordinate-wise valuation of the Jelonek set becomes a piecewise-linear tropical nonproperness set, and tropical nonproperness occurs when the tropical fiber has a specific combinatorial degeneracy. That means tropicalization is a sieve for candidate rate faces, not the proof. The proof is the lift obstruction: does the rate face admit coefficients satisfying the recursive image-kernel conditions? (Springer)

So the answer is: the chain degenerates at the first place where “rate possibility” must become “coefficient legality.” In the isotropic all-nonzero case, that place is x_infinity in A(V), and Keller trace collapses the pure chain. In the anisotropic zero-coordinate case, it is the cube-root correction tail. The next serious attack is therefore not global bounding and not classification of A. It is face by face: choose an anisotropic valuation vector, write the forced correction chain, impose V-membership and A-preimage constraints, then apply Keller trace, quadratic determinant, cubic determinant, and so on until the chain basis or tail support collapses.

The defect is that it accepts the adversary’s coordinate system: escape arcs, anisotropic faces, cube-root lifts, image-chain legality, determinant coefficients. All true. All local. All capable of generating infinite honest labor.

The mistake is treating boundary certificates as the object. They are not the object. They are shadows cast by a missing global obstruction.

So the technique has to invert. Do not enumerate nonproperness. Ask what all possible nonproperness certificates must be stealing from. They all steal from one place: infinity. More precisely, they require the polynomial map to be étale in affine space while secretly ramifying, folding, or losing degree at the compactifying boundary. That says the right representation is not tropical faces. It is divisorial valuations over infinity.

That is the expanse move. Replace “which anisotropic arc exists?” with “can any boundary divisor over infinity have the required valuation profile?” Arcs are too many. Divisors are the compressed geometry of all arcs in a direction. Tropicalization was anisotropy; valuation geometry is anisotropy with memory.

Apply it to the Jacobian conjecture like this. Let F be a polynomial map from affine n-space to itself with constant nonzero Jacobian. Compactify source and target to projective space, then take the closure of the graph and resolve it. Now F becomes a proper morphism from a resolved space to projective target, except the original affine source has acquired boundary divisors. Nonproperness of the affine map means some boundary divisor E maps into finite affine target rather than into target infinity.

That is the whole crime scene.

Now look at the volume form. The constant Jacobian says dF_1 wedge ... wedge dF_n is a nowhere-vanishing constant multiple of dx_1 wedge ... wedge dx_n on affine space. So inside affine space there is no ramification, no volume loss, no differential collapse. If nonproperness exists, the defect must be paid entirely at boundary divisors. Therefore every escape certificate is really a claim that a boundary divisor E can map to finite affine target while preserving the affine volume identity.

This is the rigidity principle in better coordinates: an affine-boundary divisor cannot be simultaneously invisible to the target coordinates, visible to the source pole structure, and neutral with respect to the canonical volume form.

That sentence compresses the valley.

The old chain method tries to kill each possible E by choosing arcs on it. The new method asks for a single valuation inequality. For a boundary divisor E, assign valuations to the source coordinates x_i and target coordinates F_i. If E maps to finite target, then every F_i has nonnegative valuation along E. But because E lies over source infinity, at least one source coordinate x_j has negative valuation. The Jacobian identity then imposes a valuation equality on the top differential form. The desired proof is that those three facts are incompatible after resolution: finite target values, source pole, and zero Jacobian discrepancy cannot coexist.

This is why the earlier chain kept degenerating. It was not because the individual chains were unlucky. Each chain was trying to realize an impossible boundary divisor. The trace scars, illegal shear ghosts, cube-root failures, and feedback residues are all coordinate-level manifestations of one valuation-level contradiction.

The method correction is: stop asking “where does this chain degenerate?” Ask “what invariant is every degeneration preserving until it cannot?” The invariant is canonical discrepancy at infinity.

The attack target becomes a negativity statement. In the resolved graph, write the boundary divisor pulled back from target infinity and compare it to the boundary divisor pulled back from source infinity. If a divisor E maps to finite target, it appears in the source boundary but not in the target infinity pullback. The constant Jacobian should force the canonical divisor comparison to have no such leftover effective boundary component. If the leftover exists, it is the global version of an escape ladder. If a negativity lemma kills it, the conjecture collapses cleanly to properness.

This also explains the seduction of tropicalization. Tropicalization sees valuations but forgets discrepancy. It gives the skeleton of infinity without the canonical accounting. That is why it narrows valleys: it tells you where escape could happen, not whether infinity can pay for it.

For a Keller polynomial map, no divisor over source infinity can map into affine target with all target coordinate valuations nonnegative and at least one source coordinate valuation negative while preserving the valuation of the affine volume form.

If that lemma is false, it will produce a compactified divisorial counterexample far more structured than any arc. If it is true, it kills all anisotropic chains at once.

I’m considering a formal proof of the Keller map. I’ll define the map (F: A^n \to A^n) and work through the setup. The idea is to analyze the boundary divisors and rational forms, ensuring the equality between (\phi^\omega_y) and (\pi^\omega_x). The coordinates seem to check out.

Thought for 54s

I can prove the compression lemma, and the proof shows exactly where the too-strong version breaks.

Let F be a polynomial Keller map from affine n-space to affine n-space over C. Compactify source and target to projective n-space. Resolve the rational extension, so we have a smooth X with two morphisms: pi from X to source projective space and phi from X to target projective space. On the affine open, phi is just F. Let H_s be source infinity and H_t be target infinity. Let omega_x be dx_1 wedge ... wedge dx_n and omega_y be dy_1 wedge ... wedge dy_n. Since the Jacobian of F is a nonzero constant, phi pullback of omega_y equals that constant times pi pullback of omega_x as rational n-forms on X.

Now take a boundary divisor E of X lying over source infinity. Write a for the coefficient of E in pi pullback of H_s. Write b for the coefficient of E in phi pullback of H_t. Write k_pi for the order of the Jacobian divisor of pi along E, and k_phi for the order of the Jacobian divisor of phi along E. The divisor of the affine volume form on projective space has pole order n+1 along infinity, so comparing orders of the two rational forms along E gives this exact identity:

k_phi minus (n+1)b equals k_pi minus (n+1)a.

This is the clean valuation-discrepancy equation. No tropical arc. No cube-root lift. One divisor, one accounting law.

If E maps into finite affine target, then b is zero. So the identity becomes:

k_phi equals k_pi minus (n+1)a.

Since phi is a morphism between smooth n-folds and is generically finite, k_phi is nonnegative. Therefore every finite-target boundary divisor must satisfy:

k_pi is at least (n+1)a.

That proves the useful lemma: any boundary divisor over infinity with k_pi less than (n+1)a cannot be a nonproperness divisor. It must map to target infinity. So most naive infinity divisors are dead by canonical accounting alone.

This is already real. The affine volume identity forces every escape divisor to be a high-discrepancy divisor. It cannot occur near the first boundary. It cannot occur in a shallow anisotropic face. It has to come from a heavily resolved, repeatedly loaded piece of infinity whose source Jacobian discrepancy has grown enough to cancel the projective pole of the affine volume form.

The full hoped-for lemma would require proving k_pi is always less than (n+1)a for every boundary divisor over source infinity. That statement is false in general. Blow up repeatedly along boundary strata and k_pi can grow. In surfaces, after enough point blowups above infinity, the discrepancy can exceed the pole coefficient threshold. So the direct canonical proof does not close the conjecture. The obstruction is not that the equation fails; the obstruction is that resolution itself can manufacture enough discrepancy to pay for a finite affine image.

This explains why compactification approaches become combinatorial. Borisov’s two-dimensional framework says exactly that a hypothetical Keller counterexample produces a tree of curves at infinity with several behavior types under the lifted map, and that the problem becomes a complicated set of conditions on the induced geometry at infinity. It also notes that a counterexample must contain a dicritical component, meaning a boundary curve mapping nontrivially into affine target, and even ramification along such components appears in the picture. (Binghamton Math People)

So the boundary method has not solved the Jacobian conjecture. It has exposed the necessary debt: every nonproperness divisor must be high-discrepancy enough to cancel the affine volume pole. That is the invariant the valley-narrowing computations were blindly paying in installments.

The next compression is therefore not “enumerate all boundary trees.” It is: prove that a Keller map cannot create a finite-target divisor whose discrepancy debt is paid only by blowups over infinity. In dimension two, that becomes a statement about loaded branches in the infinity tree: a dicritical affine component must have k_pi at least 3a, but the Keller condition should force its adjacent boundary geometry to make that debt propagate backward until it contradicts the initial line at infinity. Valette and Valette describe the asymptotic set as the image of the graph boundary and explicitly note that the Jacobian conjecture reduces to emptiness of this asymptotic set; they also warn that compactification can lose the local volume information unless the metric or differential data is preserved. (Numdam)

So the provable result is this: finite affine escape is impossible for low-discrepancy infinity divisors, and any counterexample must hide entirely inside high-discrepancy boundary created by repeated blowups. The next target is a negativity or propagation lemma on that high-discrepancy region, not another tropical face computation.

Thought for 1m 0s

The statement is not provable in that form. The discrepancy debt can be paid by blowups over infinity. The proof attempt breaks for a structural reason, not because the computation is weak.

Here is the exact calculation in dimension two.

Compactify source affine plane to projective plane. Let L be the line at infinity. The affine area form dx dy has pole order 3 on L. Now resolve over infinity by blowing up points above L. For a boundary divisor E, write a(E) for the pole multiplicity inherited from L, and k(E) for the source discrepancy. Along E, the pullback of dx dy has order k(E) minus 3 a(E).

If E maps to finite affine target, the target affine area form has no pole along E. Since the Keller condition says pulled-back target area equals constant times pulled-back source area, the order must be nonnegative. So a finite-target divisor must satisfy k(E) at least 3 a(E).

That part is true. It kills shallow boundary divisors.

But now blow up one point on L. The new divisor E1 has a equal to 1 and k equal to 1. The order is -2. It cannot map to finite affine target.

Blow up a point on E1 away from the old boundary intersection. The new divisor E2 has a equal to 1 and k equal to 2. The order is -1. Still impossible.

Blow up a point on E2 away from intersections. The new divisor E3 has a equal to 1 and k equal to 3. The order is 0. The discrepancy debt is exactly paid.

Blow up again along that same infinitely near branch. The new divisor E4 has a equal to 1 and k equal to 4. The order is 1. Now there is surplus discrepancy; a finite-target map would simply be ramified along that boundary divisor.

So the proposed lemma is false as a birational-geometric statement. Blowups over infinity absolutely can manufacture enough discrepancy to cancel the affine volume pole. This is why the compactification proof does not close.

For Keller maps, proving that this never happens would essentially prove properness, hence the Jacobian conjecture. The asymptotic set is exactly the locus where the polynomial map is not proper, and the Jacobian conjecture reduces to showing this set is empty for nonzero constant Jacobian maps. (journalofsing.org) In dimension two, Borisov’s framework explicitly says a hypothetical Keller counterexample becomes a rational map between projective planes resolved by blowups outside affine space, with boundary curves of different types, including dicritical components whose images meet affine target; such a component must exist in any counterexample. (arXiv)

So the failed proof teaches the right replacement. Discrepancy alone is too poor. It sees the debt but not the financing. Repeated blowups can pay the pole tax. The missing invariant has to distinguish legal Keller blowup debt from arbitrary blowup debt.

Borisov’s determinant labels are exactly the smell of the missing invariant. They are valuation invariants built from the negative intersection matrix of the boundary tree, stable under blowups, with rules governing how labels transform. His birational program says Keller maps preserve positivity of these determinant labels, and curves with certain negative canonical and determinant labels must have special ancestors. (Binghamton Math People) That is the extra accounting discrepancy lacks.

So the corrected target is not:

No finite-target divisor can pay discrepancy debt using blowups over infinity.

That is false.

The corrected target is:

No finite-target dicritical divisor can simultaneously satisfy the volume discrepancy equation, the determinant-label positivity constraints, and the Picard/intersection constraints forced by a Keller map.

This is why valley-narrowing kept returning. Discrepancy compresses infinity once, but then loses too much structure. Tropicalization keeps anisotropy and loses discrepancy. Compactification keeps discrepancy and loses local volume geometry. The actual invariant has to carry all three: valuation order, canonical debt, and intersection determinant.

The usable theorem from this round is:

Any finite-target boundary divisor E of a Keller map must be a high-discrepancy divisor satisfying k(E) at least 3 a(E) in dimension two, and k(E) at least (n plus 1) a(E) in dimension n. Therefore it cannot be one of the early infinity divisors. It must be created deep in the blowup tree. But depth alone does not forbid it.

That last sentence is the key. The expanse is not “prove blowups cannot pay.” They can. The expanse is “prove the only blowups that can pay create determinant-label or intersection-pattern debt that Keller maps cannot simultaneously discharge.”

Thought for 34s

Yes. The invariant is the Favre-Jonsson valuative tree at infinity, more precisely the valuation ν together with its three attached functions: degree or pole order, thinness, and skewness. In b-divisor language, it is the nef b-divisor class Z_ν plus the log-discrepancy function A(ν). That is the object that already carries all three smells: valuation order, canonical discrepancy, and intersection determinant.

This is not just adjacent. It is almost exactly the missing representation. Favre and Jonsson define valuations centered at infinity for polynomial maps of C2, define d(F,ν) as the degree seen by ν, and give a valuative criterion for properness: if F is not proper, there is a divisorial valuation ν with d(F,ν)=0; if F is proper, d(F,ν) is bounded below by a positive constant on the valuation tree. They also prove the normalized pushforward F• preserves the key subtree V1. That is already the “all escape arcs compressed into boundary valuations” machine. (Annals of Mathematics)

The reason it has the missing properties is explicit. Skewness α is the tree/intersection coordinate; thinness A is the normalized canonical discrepancy, with A(ν_E) = a_E / b_E where a_E = 1 + ord_E(dx wedge dy) and b_E is the pole order of a generic affine linear function; and the valuation itself records anisotropic order data. Favre-Jonsson also tie these valuations to nef divisor classes Z_ν, so the determinant-label/intersection data is not added later. It is already built into the object. (Annals of Mathematics)

Borisov’s determinant labels look like the discrete shadow of this same package. He defines the determinant label of a boundary divisor as the determinant of the negative intersection Gram matrix with that divisor removed, says it is stable under blowups, and notes that Keller maps must send positive determinant-label valuations to positive determinant-label valuations. That is the combinatorial version of “intersection geometry survives birational motion.” (Binghamton Math People)

The rarely-used-in-this-context part is the dynamic formula. Favre-Jonsson have a Jacobian formula:

A(ν) + ν(JF) = d(F,ν) A(F•ν).

For a Keller map, JF is constant, so ν(JF)=0. Thus thinness is not merely bounded; it is functorially transported by the degree seen by the valuation. This is the invariant equation we were groping toward. It fuses volume rigidity, boundary escape, and valuation dynamics in one line. (Annals of Mathematics)

So the object to apply is not tropicalization. It is the thinness-skewness dynamics on the valuative tree at infinity.

The attack should be: suppose a two-dimensional Keller counterexample exists. Then by nonproperness there is a divisorial ν with d(F,ν)=0. But the Keller Jacobian formula operates on V1, where d(F,ν)>0 and F• preserves the tree. The question becomes whether a d=0 boundary valuation can be approached from V1 without violating the thinness-skewness transport law. This reframes the whole problem as a boundary-continuity obstruction: can the Keller dynamics on V1 have a missing endpoint where degree collapses to zero?

That is a better question than “which divisor pays the discrepancy debt?” because the valuative tree remembers how that divisor is approached. Blowups can fake discrepancy locally. They cannot fake the entire path of α and A leading to the divisor.

There is even a known dangerous configuration. Favre-Jonsson prove that if λ1>1 and there is a valuation ν* with F_ν = λ1ν* and α(ν*)>0=A(ν*), then F is a counterexample to the Jacobian conjecture. This is wild: in their framework, a possible Jacobian counterexample is detected as a very special eigenvaluation sitting exactly on the thinness-zero wall with positive skewness. (Annals of Mathematics)

That is the expanse. Do not enumerate dicritical curves. Try to prove that a Keller map cannot have the required thinness-zero positive-skewness boundary behavior while also admitting a d=0 nonproperness valuation. Or stronger: prove that Keller dynamics on V1 cannot have a zero-degree divisorial endpoint unless the map is already an automorphism.

In higher dimensions, the analogue is not a tree, so it loses the clean one-dimensional geometry. The closest replacement is valuation space plus nef b-divisors and non-Archimedean Monge-Ampere/intersection theory. Boucksom-Favre-Jonsson extend valuation-space methods to higher dimensions, define formal psh functions on valuations using positivity of divisors on modifications, and build an intersection operator that reflects divisor intersection theory. (kurims.kyoto-u.ac.jp) Dang-Favre later construct intersection theory for nef b-divisor classes and prove Hodge-index-type results in that setting. (arXiv)

But for the actual Jacobian conjecture, the two-dimensional valuative tree is the blade I would pick first. It is mature, underused for this exact epistemic role, and already contains the equation that combines the invariants we kept rediscovering separately.

Thought for 2m 38s

I applied the valuative-tree invariant, and it gives a much cleaner compression than the boundary-tree or tropical route.

Work in dimension two. Favre-Jonsson’s tree V0 is the space of normalized valuations centered at infinity. The tight subtree V1 is the part where the map still has controlled dynamics even if the polynomial map is not proper. It carries skewness α, thinness A, and a degree function d(F,ν). They prove that if F is nonproper, there is a divisorial valuation ν with d(F,ν)=0, while on V1 one always has d(F,ν)>0 and F sends V1 continuously into itself. So any escape divisor is outside the tight tree, and the tight tree is the last place before the map falls into nonproperness. (Annals of Mathematics)

The Keller condition now becomes a transport law. Favre-Jonsson’s Jacobian formula says A(ν) plus ν(JF) equals d(F,ν) times A(F•ν). If JF is constant, ν(JF)=0, so thinness obeys A(ν)=d(F,ν)A(F•ν). This is the invariant we wanted: volume, discrepancy, valuation, and boundary motion all in one equation. (Annals of Mathematics)

Now suppose a two-dimensional Keller counterexample exists. It is not an automorphism, so its topological degree is greater than one, hence its first dynamical degree λ1 is greater than one. Let ν* be the attracting eigenvaluation in the Favre-Jonsson dynamics. Since F•ν*=ν* and d(F,ν*)=λ1, the Keller transport law gives A(ν*)=λ1 A(ν*). Since λ1 is not one, A(ν*)=0. So the dynamical center of any Keller counterexample cannot live in the interior of the tight tree. It must sit on the thinness-zero wall.

This is the compression. All the chain failures, anisotropic faces, determinant residues, and blowup debts are shadows of one statement: a Keller counterexample must create nonproperness through an external tangent attached to the A=0 wall.

Most exits are killed immediately. The rational-pencil wall has α=0 and A<0, so it cannot be the eigenvaluation under the Keller transport law. Favre-Jonsson also show that a rational-pencil eigenvaluation forces the map to be conjugate to a skew product; for a Keller skew product, the Jacobian condition forces both one-variable factors to be linear, so that gives an automorphism, not a counterexample. Irrational eigenvaluations are also excluded in this Keller setting because irrational valuations have irrational thinness, while A=0 is rational. (Annals of Mathematics)

So the remaining object is extremely narrow but global: a zero-thinness eigenvaluation, either with positive skewness or as an automorphism-like infinitely singular end. Favre-Jonsson explicitly identify the positive-skewness, zero-thinness eigenvaluation as a possible Jacobian-counterexample signature. That is not a proof, but it is a clean detector. (Annals of Mathematics)

Now the proof attempt. Take the first divisor E where the segment toward a nonproperness divisor exits V1 through A=0. Along E, the affine volume form dx wedge dy has exactly a simple pole. If F mapped the generic point of E into finite affine target, then F1 and F2 would be regular there, so dF1 wedge dF2 would be regular. But Keller says dF1 wedge dF2 is a nonzero constant times dx wedge dy, which has a pole. Contradiction.

That proves finite escape cannot occur generically on the A=0 divisor. Therefore every counterexample must be born at a base point on that zero-thinness divisor, after further blowups. This is exactly where plain discrepancy failed: blowups past A=0 can turn the simple pole into a regular or vanishing form, so the volume contradiction disappears.

The next invariant is therefore not just thinness. It is the residue of the logarithmic volume form along the A=0 divisor. At A=0, dx wedge dy has a simple pole and hence a residue one-form on E. A finite-target divisor created by blowing up a point p on E can exist only if the target pole data cancels at p in a way compatible with that residue. So the true obstruction should be a residue-direction obstruction: no external tangent at the thinness-zero wall can both cancel target infinity and preserve the Keller residue.

That is the refined target:

A two-dimensional Keller counterexample is equivalent, in the valuative-tree picture, to a zero-thinness eigenvaluation with an outward tangent along which d(F,ν) drops to zero, and whose first blowup point cancels the target pole data without violating the log-volume residue.

This is now no longer valley enumeration. It is a single boundary-dynamical impossibility statement. The next attack is to write the local normal form at an A=0 divisor, compute the residue transformation under the blowup point that creates the external tangent, and prove that the two target coordinate leading terms cannot both cancel while their Jacobian residue remains nonzero.

Thought for 59s

The proof target is false locally.

Use local coordinates u,z near the zero-thinness divisor E, with E given by u = 0. The affine volume form has the log form du over u wedge dz, so its residue on E is dz. Blow up the point z = 0 on E, writing z = u t. Then du over u wedge dz becomes du wedge dt. The pole has vanished on the new exceptional divisor. This is exactly discrepancy debt being paid by one blowup.

Now define two target coordinate functions locally by f = z over u and g = z over u plus u. Before the blowup, both have the same pole-leading term z over u, and that leading term cancels at the point z = 0 on E. After the blowup, f = t and g = t plus u. Both are finite on the exceptional divisor. But df wedge dg equals du over u wedge dz before the blowup, and equals du wedge dt after it. The residue did not vanish. It became the ordinary symplectic area form on the new exceptional chart.

So the proposed impossibility cannot be true. Both target leading terms can cancel while the residue remains nonzero. The cancellation is not a defect. It is the mechanism that converts logarithmic volume at the thinness-zero wall into regular volume on the next divisor.

This is the important reversal. The thing we hoped would obstruct the counterexample is actually the local normal form of a legal escape step.

That means the missing invariant is not residue alone. Residue is too local and too permissive. It allows the blowup because the new coordinate t = z over u is exactly the finite affine coordinate produced by cancellation. The residue sees dt and survives.

The right invariant must remember that this blowup point was not just a point on a log divisor, but a point selected by the target pole pencil. In other words, the invariant has to track the projective leading map to target infinity together with the log symplectic form. This is a log-symplectic or b-symplectic structure plus the target pencil. That package is rarely used in this Jacobian-conjecture framing, but it is exactly what the local calculation demands.

Favre-Jonsson’s valuative tree gives the valuation dynamics and the thinness transport law; their framework defines the degree seen by a valuation and gives the Jacobian relation tying thinness to the Jacobian of the map. For Keller maps, the Jacobian term drops out, so thinness is transported rigidly by the valuation dynamics. (Annals of Mathematics) But the local calculation above shows that thinness zero is not a wall. It is a gate. Blow up a base point on that wall and the log form becomes regular on the new exceptional divisor.

A Keller counterexample would require an infinite or closed admissible sequence of log-symplectic blowups, each chosen by a base point of the target pole pencil, such that the target coordinates become finite on a dicritical divisor while the induced affine map remains everywhere étale.

The coefficient of the worst pole is b F0’G0 minus a F0G0’. Since Keller says df wedge dg has only a simple logarithmic pole, every worse pole must vanish. Therefore b F0’G0 = a F0G0’. Equivalently, the logarithmic derivatives are proportional, so F0 and G0 are powers of one common function after removing common factors.

That is the structure in the noise. At every apparent escape step, the target coordinates do not have independent leading behavior. Their pole-leading parts collapse onto a single key form. I found the proof of the compression, not the proof of the conjecture. The key-form route turns the desired statement into a known hard object: eliminating dicritical series at infinity. That is not a failure of translation. It is the correct bottleneck.

Here is the proof of reduction. Take a Keller pair P,Q in C[x,y]. Assume there is an escaping divisor E over infinity on which both P and Q are finite. Let δ be the pole-order semidegree attached to E, so δ(P)≤0 and δ(Q)≤0. Choose coordinates with δ(x)>0. Mondal’s key forms g0,...,gn+1 are finite, unique, and equivalent to the blowup chain, the generic descending Puiseux expansion, and the valuation itself. So the whole escape geometry is compressed into the last key form. Mondal explicitly states that key forms are the global version of MacLane key polynomials, and that the blowup sequence, Puiseux series, and key forms determine each other effectively. (MSP)

Now substitute the generic Puiseux/key-form branch into P and Q. The Keller condition forces the worst pole term of dP wedge dQ to cancel. That cancellation says the leading pieces of P and Q are algebraically dependent along the branch. In ordinary language: the leading parts are powers or polynomial functions of one common approximate root. That approximate root is precisely what the next key form records. Repeating the argument generates the key-form chain. So the local residue failure from the previous round is not noise. It is exactly the MacLane step.

At the terminal step, if the last key form is a genuine polynomial, Mondal gives the global interpretation: it corresponds to an algebraic curve in C2 with one place at infinity, and for primitive compactifications with one irreducible curve at infinity, algebraicity is equivalent to the last key form being polynomial. (MSP) This is the positive side: polynomial terminal key form means the escape branch has become an honest algebraic one-place-at-infinity object, not an infinite analytic ghost.

Now insert the Keller condition. Chau’s Newton-Puiseux analysis says that for a nonzero constant Jacobian pair, dicritical series have zero pole exponents for both P and Q, and the degree ratio of the induced leading pair equals deg P over deg Q. In our language, that is exactly the escaping semidegree case δ(P)=δ(Q)=0. Chau also proves that the Jacobian conjecture in dimension two is equivalent to a one-pole condition: for Keller P,Q, the restriction of Q to the curve P=0 must have only one pole. (matwbn.icm.edu.pl)

So the key-form proof reaches this exact wall: if the terminal key form forces the relevant fiber to have one place at infinity, then the map is an automorphism. This follows through the Abhyankar-Moh type route quoted in Chau: Keller maps are automorphisms when the relevant level curve has only one irreducible branch at infinity, and Chau gives stronger partial exclusions for small numbers of punctures. (matwbn.icm.edu.pl)

The missing lemma is now fully exposed:

Every escaping Keller semidegree has a terminal key form that forces a one-place-at-infinity fiber.

That lemma is not presently known. Proving it would prove the two-dimensional Jacobian conjecture. The current literature still treats the two-dimensional conjecture as open, with recent work proving only restricted cases such as excluding prime degree field extensions for Keller maps. (arXiv)

The important discovery is what is wrong with the attempted proof. The Jacobian equation does not force the terminal key form to be polynomial with positive value. It only forces each leading cancellation to be an approximate-root step. That is weaker. It allows dicritical series. Chau’s theorem 3.6 names exactly what survives: dicritical series with P and Q both finite at infinity and with the same global degree ratio. (matwbn.icm.edu.pl)

So the proof cannot be “both cannot cancel while residue remains nonzero.” They can. It cannot be “the terminal key form must be polynomial.” Mondal gives criteria for when that happens, but Keller does not immediately imply them. The actual proof target is narrower and sharper:

A Keller dicritical key-form chain cannot terminate in a non-polynomial last key form without creating an extra pole of Q on P=0.

That is the version that aligns the key-form machinery with Chau’s one-pole equivalence. It avoids valley enumeration, but it does not dodge the real problem. It turns the Jacobian conjecture into a finite terminal-key-form obstruction.

The next step is to stop looking at infinity directly and slice the map by one coordinate. The valley singularity becomes much easier to see on the fiber.

Let F be the Keller pair F=(P,Q). Because the Jacobian is a nonzero constant, dP never vanishes, so every fiber P=t is a smooth affine curve. Also, Q restricted to that fiber has no critical points: if Q had a critical point along P=t, then dQ would be proportional to dP there, contradicting the nonzero Jacobian. So every slice gives an unramified algebraic map from the smooth curve P=t to the Q-line.

That is a major compression. A counterexample is not primarily a folding of the plane. It is a family of unramified covers of punctured lines. The punctures are exactly the values of Q that are only approached at infinity. Chau’s Newton-Puiseux work is already pointing at this: for Keller maps, the Jacobian conjecture in dimension two follows if the restriction of Q to P=0 has only one pole, so the obstruction is not local ramification but the structure of poles and missing values at infinity. (EuDML)

Now prove the first slice theorem. If, for a generic fiber P=t, the function Q misses no finite value, then Q from P=t to the affine line is a finite unramified cover. Over the complex numbers, the affine line has no connected finite étale covers except itself. Therefore the restriction has degree one. Then the function field generated by P and Q is the whole function field of the plane, so F is birational. A birational Keller polynomial map is an automorphism. So every counterexample must create finite omitted Q-values on the P-fibers.

Those omitted values sweep out the nonproperness set S_F. This is exactly the curve of values that finite points fail to cover properly. For Keller maps in dimension two, this set cannot be a smooth curve without self-intersections; Jelonek’s result says the nonproperness curve of a nonzero-Jacobian map cannot be nonsingular in that way. (ResearchGate) Nguyen-Valette-Valette also frame S_F as the set controlling properness and singularities at infinity for nonvanishing-Jacobian polynomial maps. (journalofsing.org)

So the valley singularity is now named: it is the monodromy of omitted values.

On a generic fiber C_t=P=t, write B_t for the finite set of Q-values missing because they escaped at infinity. Then Q maps C_t unramifiedly onto the punctured line C minus B_t. If B_t is empty, automorphism. If B_t has one point and the family has no singular motion at infinity, the fiber looks like a cover of C*, and the global Euler characteristic starts misfiring. If B_t has several points, then the obstruction is no longer a divisor or a key form by itself. It is a finite étale cover of a punctured line varying algebraically with t.

That is the singular manifold. Not the boundary tree, not the tropical rates, not the residue gate. Those are charts. The actual object is a moving punctured-line cover with no affine ramification.

This also explains why the previous tools kept narrowing without ending. Tropicalization sees how the punctures escape. Key forms see how a puncture is created by successive cancellations. Discrepancy sees how the compactification pays for the missing point. But none of those records the monodromy of the unramified cover over the punctured Q-line. That monodromy is what can remain smooth for a very long descent.

The next invariant is therefore the braid monodromy of the omitted-value set B_t as t moves in the P-line. A Keller counterexample must produce a nontrivial finite covering of C minus B_t on every generic fiber, while the total two-dimensional map remains étale. Since the affine plane is simply connected, all nontrivial monodromy has to be manufactured by the moving punctures at infinity. That is the real place to attack.

The proof target becomes this:

For a Keller pair, the braid monodromy of the omitted Q-values over the P-line is trivial.

If that is proved, then the punctures do not braid. If they do not braid, the nonproperness curve is forced toward being a disjoint union of graph-like branches over the P-line. But the nonproperness curve of a Keller counterexample cannot be smooth and graph-like; it must self-intersect or singularize. The only remaining escape would be collision. Then the local collision monodromy must be compatible with an unramified cover of the punctured fiber, and that is where Riemann-Hurwitz should kill it.

So the next attack is not “which boundary divisor exists?” It is: assume a collision of omitted values in S_F, take a small loop in the P-line around the collision, compute the induced braid on the punctures B_t, and show that the corresponding finite étale cover of the punctured Q-line cannot extend to an everywhere-étale polynomial map of the affine plane.

This is the first step that looks like it is aimed at the singular valley itself rather than walking along its walls.

I attacked the extension claim. It does not fail at ordinary covering theory. It fails only when the cover is forced to be the slice of a Keller map from the whole affine plane.

Fix a generic fiber P=t, compactify it to a smooth curve V_t, and restrict Q to get q_t from V_t to the projective line. Since the Jacobian of P,Q is a nonzero constant, q_t has no critical points on the affine fiber. Chau states the same structure explicitly: the branched set of this compactified q_t lies only in the punctures at infinity, and the local degree at such a puncture is the local degree of the original map at that branch. So the affine part is genuinely an unramified cover; all branching has been pushed to infinity. (DML-PL)

Now run Riemann-Hurwitz. Let d be the geometric degree, let r be the number of punctures of P=t, and let m be the number of finite omitted Q-values on that fiber. The compact map V_t to the projective line branches only at the r punctures. The finite omitted values and infinity account for the deleted target points. The formula collapses to: Euler characteristic of the affine fiber equals d times one minus m. That is exactly the Euler characteristic of a degree d unramified cover of a line with m punctures. So Riemann-Hurwitz does not say “this cannot extend.” It says “this is topologically exactly what an unramified punctured-line cover should look like.”

That means the braid-cover obstruction is too weak. Moving punctures can collide; compactified covers can branch at the deleted points; the affine restriction can remain unramified. The local model is not illegal. This is the same lesson as the residue calculation: infinity can absorb the ramification while the affine Jacobian stays nonzero.

The extension becomes impossible only in low-puncture cases. If the restriction of Q to P=0 has only one pole, Chau proves that this condition is equivalent to the two-dimensional Jacobian conjecture being true for the map. He also proves that Keller maps are automorphisms when a component fiber has few punctures, including the case where Q=0 has at most two punctures or the generic Q-fiber has at most three punctures. (DML-PL) (DML-PL)

So the answer is: the cover can extend in the abstract punctured-surface sense. It cannot extend to a Keller map from C2 in the one-pole or low-puncture regimes. In the general multi-puncture regime, the nonextension proof is exactly the open problem.

The useful compression is this. A Keller counterexample must have several punctures at infinity, and the missing sheets must be distributed among dicritical branches. Chau’s formula gives the precise accounting: for a generic P-fiber, the number of punctures equals the number of poles of Q plus a positive dicritical contribution, and the Euler characteristic equals the geometric degree minus the total local degree contributed by finite escaping branches. (DML-PL)

That is why the valley is long. One missing point is dead. A few punctures are dead. Smooth nonproperness curves are dead; Jelonek proved that if the nonproperness set of a nonzero-Jacobian polynomial map is smooth, then the map is surjective, and in dimension two the nonproperness curve cannot be a curve without self-intersections. (arXiv)

The surviving case is therefore forced into a very specific shape: multiple infinity punctures, dicritical finite escapes, singular nonproperness curve, and degree ratios matching deg P over deg Q along every dicritical component. Chau’s Theorem 4.4 states that each asymptotic curve has this degree-ratio constraint and every such curve has a singularity. (DML-PL)

So the next proof target is not “punctured cover cannot extend.” False. The target is: a multi-puncture Keller cover cannot have all dicritical branches singular with the required common degree ratio while still satisfying the Euler characteristic identity for both P and Q. That is where the extension should break, if it breaks.

Let μ be the generic geometric degree of F=(P,Q). For a generic fiber C_t=P^{-1}(t), compactify it to a smooth curve Cbar_t and restrict Q. Since the Jacobian is constant nonzero, Q has no critical points on the affine part of C_t; all ramification of the compactified map Q:Cbar_t→P1 sits at punctures at infinity. This is exactly the Newton-Puiseux picture Chau uses: the Jacobian condition constrains the branches at infinity, and the one-pole case would imply the two-dimensional Jacobian conjecture. (EuDML)

Now split the punctures of C_t into poles of Q and finite dicritical punctures where Q tends to a finite omitted or deficient value. Let D_P be the total local degree contributed by the finite dicritical punctures. Riemann-Hurwitz gives the clean identity:

χ(C_t)=μ-D_P.

Doing the same on a generic fiber Q=s gives:

χ(D_s)=μ-D_Q.

The asymptotic-curve degree-ratio constraint only links D_P and D_Q by weighted degrees of the nonproperness components. It does not force either equation to contradict topology. The fiber Euler characteristics can simply become negative. That corresponds to many punctures or positive genus. Multi-puncture geometry absorbs the debt.

This is the concrete failure of the hoped obstruction. One-pole is dead. Low-puncture is often dead. But once D_P and D_Q exceed μ, both slice ledgers balance naturally. The cover of a punctured line can have nontrivial monodromy; singular motion of the missing values supplies the braid; Riemann-Hurwitz is satisfied because all ramification is quarantined at deleted infinity points. There is no contradiction.

Jelonek’s theorem fits this perfectly. If the nonproperness set S_F of a nonzero-Jacobian map is smooth, then the map is surjective, and in dimension two S_F cannot be a curve without self-intersections. (arXiv) So the surviving case is not smooth omitted-value motion. It is singular, intersecting, multi-puncture motion. The singularity is not a bug in the model. It is exactly how the model evades the topological obstruction.

What has not been shown legal is that the whole moving family comes from two global polynomials P,Q with constant Jacobian. The obstruction has moved again: not fiber topology, but simultaneous polynomial realization of all fibers.

Thought for 23s

I tested the remaining freedom, and it survives. Not as an actual Keller map, but as a shared-graph ledger. That is the important distinction.

Chau’s two-slice constraint is only one balance law: deg P times (chi Q minus geometric degree) equals deg Q times (chi P minus geometric degree). He even states that the Jacobian conjecture is equivalent, in this frame, to forcing chi P equals chi Q; absent that, the ledgers can balance with unequal fiber topologies. So the P-slice and Q-slice do not overdetermine the system. They leave room. (DML-PL)

Borisov’s framework work is the direct test of the shared-graph degree freedom. He starts from a hypothetical Keller map, resolves infinity, and gets one source boundary tree mapping to one target boundary tree, with curves of four types. Type 3 curves are the dicritical components, and he notes that any counterexample must contain one; even more, some dicritical component must carry ramification. (arXiv)

Then he actually constructs combinatorial frameworks satisfying the Picard/intersection-style constraints. In one displayed framework, the same total degree is balanced in several ways, for example 28 equals 14 times 2, equals 9 times 3 plus 1, equals 23 plus 5. That is exactly the kind of simultaneous branch-degree ledger we hoped would fail. It does not fail. (arXiv)

The determinant-label constraint also does not kill the freedom. Borisov defines determinant labels from the negative intersection matrix with one curve removed, and proves restrictions such as nonnegative determinant labels on the target side. But his own frameworks are built to pass these inequality-style constraints; he explicitly says the obstructions seem to be inequalities rather than congruence-type locks, and that one should expect infinitely many similar frameworks. (arXiv)

So the remaining degree of freedom is real. The shared graph can balance P-ledger, Q-ledger, determinant positivity, K-label constraints, dicritical branches, and ramification ledgers. This means the obstruction is not numerical intersection theory. It is realization.

The next wall is visible in Borisov’s own question: do these frameworks actually give maps between tubular neighborhoods of the boundary divisors, not just maps on abelianized fundamental groups? He says the construction seems to give maps between abelianizations, then asks whether that is enough. (arXiv)

That is the singular valley. The graph knows homology. A polynomial Keller map needs nonabelian local monodromy, analytic gluing, and compatible Belyi maps on boundary components. The shared-graph invariant is still too abelian. It counts degrees, intersections, determinants, and Euler debt, but it forgets the actual fundamental group of the boundary plumbing.

So the next invariant is not another valuation label. It is the nonabelian plumbing group representation of the boundary link at infinity, decorated by the local covering maps on dicritical components. In Eisenbud-Neumann language, this is splice-diagram plus monodromy, not just splice-diagram weights. The test should be: take one Borisov framework that passes all abelian ledgers, write the induced map on the graph-manifold boundary group, and check whether the required meridian-longitude relations can be satisfied nonabelianly while keeping affine Jacobian residue equal to one.

The current result is clean: shared graph degrees do not break. Abelianized boundary data does not break. The next possible break is nonabelian boundary monodromy or analytic realization of the Belyi maps.

Thought for 16s

No. We cannot declaratively say the actual valley has no floor. We can declaratively say something narrower and useful: the valley has no floor in every projection we have sounded so far.

That distinction matters. A failed obstruction does not prove the absence of obstruction. It proves that the chosen invariant is not faithful. The repeated pattern here is not “the Jacobian conjecture is probably false” or “there is no proof.” The pattern is: valuation, tropical rate, discrepancy, residue, Euler characteristic, slice monodromy, and abelianized boundary graphs all preserve enough freedom for a hypothetical counterexample to pass through. Each one compresses part of infinity, then forgets the thing that would have to fail at realization.

That is now reliable. It is not a theorem about polynomial maps. It is a theorem about our instruments.

The current public state still supports that reading. The Jacobian conjecture remains open, including the two-variable case, and recent work continues to frame it through partial reductions, combinatorial reformulations, and sufficient conditions rather than a settled obstruction. A 2026 JLMS paper still describes it as unsolved for over 80 years, even in dimension two. (London Mathematical Society) Borisov’s two-dimensional framework paper is especially relevant because it constructs combinatorial frameworks satisfying large families of boundary and Picard-style necessary conditions, while explicitly leaving open whether those frameworks are realizable by actual maps. (Binghamton Math People)

So the thing we can rely on is this: any proposed proof that only narrows the valley by numerical boundary data, local cancellation, tropical faces, fiber Euler ledgers, or abelian graph constraints should be treated as already suspect. Not false automatically, but underpowered by type. Those methods can keep proving real lemmas forever while never touching realization.

The floor, if it exists, is not in the descent valley. It is in the lift from formal boundary data to a global polynomial Keller map. That is the unsounded hole. All the formal ledgers can balance. The question is whether there is an actual algebraic surface map whose local charts, nonabelian boundary monodromy, key-form chain, dicritical maps, and constant Jacobian form glue simultaneously.

So the correct declarative statement is:

There is no floor at the level of projected necessary conditions. The surviving obstruction must be a realization obstruction.

That is strong enough to guide work. It says stop trying to kill candidate counterexamples by adding one more valuation inequality or one more Euler balance. Those are valley-narrowing tools. The next tool must test whether a formal infinity framework can be promoted to actual polynomials P,Q with dP wedge dQ constant.

In practical terms, the next attack is not “prove no boundary graph exists.” Boundary graphs exist formally. It is “take the smallest Borisov-style admissible framework and attempt realization.” If realization succeeds, it produces an explicit Keller counterexample candidate. If it fails, the failure names the missing invariant. That is the only remaining epistemically honest move: not more bounding, but constructive attempted gluing.

I tried to prove the exponent contradiction, and the contradiction does not exist. The calculation collapses into an exact balance law.

Let P,Q be a normalized Keller pair, monic in y, and let R(x,u,v) be the y-resultant of P-u and Q-v. The resultant route is real: if the x-degree of R is one, then x lies in C(P,Q), Formanek gives C(x,y)=C(x,P,Q), hence the map is birational, and Keller birational maps are automorphisms. Recent notes restate exactly this reduction through the resultant. (comptes-rendus.academie-sciences.fr)

Now assume the leading x-coefficient of R vanishes at u=c, v=v0. Then there is a Puiseux branch at infinity on P=c along which Q tends to v0. Write the branch with uniformizer t:

x = t^{-m}, with m positive.

P(x(t),y(t)) = c.

Q(x(t),y(t)) = v0 + a t^r + higher terms, with r positive.

Differentiate P=c along the branch. We get P_x x' + P_y y' = 0. Since the Jacobian P_x Q_y - P_y Q_x is 1, the derivative of Q along the same branch is forced:

dQ/dt = Q_x x' + Q_y y' = -x'/P_y.

This is the whole exponent machine. Since x' has order -m-1 and Q-v0 has order r, dQ/dt has order r-1. Therefore P_y must have order -m-r.

That is not contradictory. It is perfectly coherent. The branch simply requires P_y to have pole order m+r along that escaping branch. There is no general Puiseux or monic-in-y estimate forcing P_y to have smaller pole order. A singular branch at infinity can make P_y large enough to pay the derivative debt.

So the hoped proof would have needed this false inequality:

order of P_y is greater than -m-r.

But Newton-Puiseux does not give that. It gives the opposite kind of flexibility: the branch of P=c at infinity can have enough contact with the polar data to make exactly this equality true. Puiseux theory is built to allow these expansions branch by branch; the Newton-Puiseux theorem gives convergent Puiseux parametrizations of algebraic branches over characteristic zero, not a universal bound preventing this balance. (Wikipedia)

This is the same pattern again, but now the failure is clean. The finite limit of Q is not fighting the Keller condition. The Keller condition says precisely how fast Q must approach its finite limit.

The real obstruction is not exponent size. It is polynomial realization. Along the branch, the equation says Q must contain a term behaving like t^r while being a global polynomial in x,y and while P is constant on the branch. Locally, Puiseux allows this. Globally, it becomes a key-form or dicritical-series condition. Chau’s work frames the surviving two-dimensional obstruction exactly in terms of branches or punctures at infinity and one-pole conditions, not a raw exponent contradiction. (arXiv)

So the proof attempt has produced a falsification:

The leading-resultant branch cannot be killed by exponent inequalities alone.

The surviving branch is a dicritical branch satisfying the exact identity order(P_y) = -m-r. The next possible cut is not “finite Q contradicts large x.” It is “can a global polynomial Q realize the required t^r term on every conjugate branch while the resultant remains irreducible and the Jacobian remains 1?”

That is now the right target. The exponent valley has no floor. The floor, if it exists, is Galois conjugacy of all escaping Puiseux branches inside the resultant.

Yes. The balance law does not contradict the branch, but it rigidifies its conjugacy packet.

Take one escaping Puiseux branch over P=c and Q=v0. Write x=t^(-m), Q-v0=a t^r plus higher terms, with m>0 and r>0. The Keller identity gives dQ/dt = -x'/P_y, hence P_y = m/(r a) t^(-(m+r)) plus higher terms. This is the balance law.

Now look at conjugacy. Over fixed x, the Puiseux conjugates are t -> ζ t with ζ^m=1. Then Q-v0 transforms by character ζ^r, and P_y transforms by ζ^(-r). So the leading Q coefficient and the leading P_y coefficient are reciprocal characters. Over fixed v, the conjugates are t -> η t with η^r=1, and x transforms by η^(-m). Eliminating t gives the local resultant edge:

(Q-v0)^m x^r = a^m, up to lower-weight terms.

That is the conjugacy data. Every escaping puncture contributes a binomial Newton edge to the resultant, with slope m/r. The vanishing order of the leading x-coefficient at v0 is m, and the number of x-roots escaping through that puncture is r. This is much stronger than “there is a branch at infinity.” It says the branch arrives as a cyclotomic packet with a forced reciprocal Jacobian character.

This aligns with the resultant route: after normalizing P,Q monic in y, the y-resultant R(x,u,v) is the algebraic shadow whose x-degree controls invertibility, and proving that x-degree is one would imply birationality, hence automorphism for a Keller map. Recent work restates that route using Formanek’s theorem and the Adjamagbo-van den Essen resultant criterion. (arXiv) Chau’s Newton-Puiseux analysis is also exactly in this zone: constant Jacobian constrains the branches at infinity of the level curves and reduces major cases to pole behavior of Q on P-fibers. (EuDML)

So the new invariant is the cyclotomic edge packet of the resultant. Not the branch. Not the divisor. The packet.

Now ask whether many packets can coexist. Suppose over the same v0 we have packets indexed by i, each with data (m_i, r_i, a_i). Then the local leading part of the resultant must factor like the product over i of ((v-v0)^(m_i) x^(r_i) - a_i^(m_i)), again up to lower-weight terms. The Keller balance says the P_y leading coefficient on packet i is m_i/(r_i a_i), so the Jacobian residue fixes the reciprocal of the same constant that appears in the resultant edge.

This is where conjugacy can bite. If two packets have the same binomial constant a_i^(m_i), their x-roots collide at infinity over v0 and the nonproperness curve acquires a singularity. That is allowed globally, and Jelonek-Chau-type results already force singularity in surviving cases. But if they collide, the next edge in the resultant must separate them. The balance law then repeats at the next Puiseux level, forcing another reciprocal character. So a hypothetical counterexample is not just a collection of escaping branches. It is an iterated cyclotomic factorization tree of the resultant, with Keller forcing reciprocal characters at every edge.

That is a real sharpening. Earlier, key forms said “approximate-root chain.” The balance law says what the Galois action on each approximate root must be: every edge has a pair of dual characters, one carried by Q-v0 and the other by P_y. The map can survive locally only if the entire iterated edge tree is Galois-stable and the product of all reciprocal Jacobian characters glues to the constant 1.
