I attacked it, and the statement is not provable at the level stated. The edge tree plus reciprocal character law is still too weak. It is compatible with irreducibility.

The smallest model already shows the break. Take the local resultant shadow R=x^2 v - 1. It is irreducible in C[x,v]. Its leading coefficient in x is v, so it vanishes at v=0. The branch at v=0 has x going to infinity: set v=t^2 and x=t^(-1). This is a nontrivial cyclotomic packet: t goes to minus t exchanges the two x-branches, while v is fixed. It has exactly the edge law v x^2 = 1. The reciprocal Jacobian character can be assigned consistently: if Q-v0 behaves like t^2, then P_y must behave like t^(-3), up to the forced scalar. Nothing in irreducibility forbids this.

This does not produce a Keller counterexample. It proves something more diagnostic: the proposed invariant is not strong enough to certify invertibility. Irreducibility of the resultant permits exactly the kind of escaping cyclotomic packet we wanted to ban.

The same failure persists for iterated trees. Newton-Puiseux theory says an irreducible plane curve germ is encoded by successive Puiseux edges; irreducibility is not “no branching,” it is organized branching. The Galois packet is the normal form of irreducibility, not its enemy. That is why the statement reverses: an irreducible resultant naturally wants a single transitive Puiseux packet. Puiseux theory guarantees convergent fractional expansions for algebraic branches over characteristic zero, so the mere presence of a cyclotomic edge tree is ordinary algebraic-curve behavior, not a Keller contradiction. (Wikipedia)

The Keller condition adds the balance law, but the balance law only says the polar data is dual to the escaping coordinate data. Kuo and Parusinski’s work on Newton-Puiseux roots of Jacobian determinants is relevant here: for a pair of functions, the tree model records contact orders, but the way polar roots split away is not determined by the tree alone. In their words, this contrasts with the one-function case. So a Puiseux tree, even decorated with Jacobian-factor behavior, is not a complete invariant of the pair. (arXiv)

So the exact claim fails as an invariant statement. To make it true, one needs an extra condition not visible in the resultant edge tree. The missing condition is not irreducibility of the resultant. So the next correct theorem is not:

It is:

For a nonproper Keller map, there exists a rational target curve gamma whose punctured monodromy image is intransitive.

That statement is much more plausible and much closer to Jędrzejewicz’s squarefree-versus-irreducible formulation: Keller preserves squarefreeness of irreducible pullbacks, while automorphism is equivalent to preserving irreducibility. (Cambridge University Press & Assessment)

The cyclic cover was the zero-frequency projection. The real object is the full nonabelian Fourier decomposition of the monodromy cover. The valley did not vanish; we chose too small a frequency basis.

The theorem is true, but only in the source-visible sense. That makes it less like a proof engine and more like a diagnostic of what information is missing.

Assume a noninvertible Keller map F exists, with degree d greater than one. Let S be its nonproperness curve and U be the complement of S in the target. Over U, F is a finite étale cover with transitive monodromy G on d sheets. This is the standard nonproperness framing: the Jacobian conjecture reduces to showing the asymptotic or nonproperness set is empty. (Journal of Singularities)

Now choose a generic affine line L in the source. Its image C = closure of F(L) is an irreducible rational curve in the target, and C is not contained in S. Let h define C. Then h(P,Q) vanishes on L, so the linear equation of L divides h(P,Q). Since d is greater than one, a generic point of C lying in U has more sheets over it than just the one coming from L. Thus h(P,Q) has another component. Keller ensures the pullback is squarefree, not irreducible; this is exactly Jędrzejewicz’s characterization: Keller maps pull irreducibles back to squarefree polynomials, while automorphisms pull irreducibles back to irreducibles. So yes: there exists a rational target curve C and a maximal intransitive subgroup H of G such that the restricted monodromy of C minus C∩S lands in H. Take H to be the stabilizer of the sheet-component containing L. The local inertia powers around C∩S preserve that sheet subset because the component L already lifts through the cover. The Fourier packet closes. The pullback splits.

But this proof cheats in exactly the way we care about. It uses a source line. It proves the witness exists after knowing a sheet upstairs. It does not construct C from S, G, inertia, or target-side data. So the theorem is existentially true and epistemically weak.

The strengthened theorem would be: from S and its inertia data alone, one can find such a rational C. That is not established, and it is where the real difficulty lives. Generic target curves will usually regenerate full transitive monodromy, so the useful C is special. It is not merely tangent to S in the right congruence classes; it must be the projection of a source-side curve, or imitate one.

This exposes the missing invariant cleanly. The nonabelian Fourier search over subgroups H is not looking for existence of reducible pullback curves. They exist in any non-birational case. It is looking for a target-intrinsic way to recover one without already knowing the upstairs sheet.

That is the object to study. Not all H. Not all rational curves. The “visible” H are those whose intermediate covers contain rational curves coming from source A2. The valley floor is no longer monodromy existence; it is source-shadow detection from target-side singular geometry.

Let F=(P,Q) with Jacobian 1. Let M(T,u,v) be the monic irreducible equation of the source coordinate x over C(P,Q), so M(x,P,Q)=0. This is the right primitive object because Formanek’s theorem gives C(x,y)=C(P,Q,x) for Keller maps, so deg_T M=1 would imply birationality, hence polynomial invertibility. The resultant route to the plane conjecture is precisely to force this degree down to one. (Comptes Rendus)

Differentiate M(x,P,Q)=0. Since the Jacobian is 1, the chain rule gives the exact identities:

M_u(x,P,Q) = -Q_y M_T(x,P,Q)

M_v(x,P,Q) = P_y M_T(x,P,Q)

This is the core law of the source-shadow web. It says the primitive-coordinate discriminant M_T divides both target partial derivatives after pullback. So a nontrivial web can only survive if every place where the T-model wants to branch is absorbed as a singularity of the hypersurface M=0 itself.

That is the escape hatch. If M_T(x,P,Q)=0 at an affine source point, then the two identities force M_u(x,P,Q)=M_v(x,P,Q)=0 too. Since M(x,P,Q)=0 already, the point (T,u,v)=(x,P,Q) is singular on the hypersurface M=0. Thus Keller does not say “the web cannot branch.” It says “the web can branch only where the primitive-coordinate model is nonnormal or singular.”

If the hypersurface M=0 were normal enough, this would kill the web. Over the target complement U=C2 minus S, the map is étale. A normal finite model over U with M_T=0 would give genuine ramification, impossible. So under the extra condition that the minimal-coordinate hypersurface has no conductor swallowing the discriminant over U, deg_T M must be one.

But that extra condition is exactly what we do not have. The normalization of M=0 is the source surface; the singular plane model can hide the discriminant in its conductor. That is the floor. The nontrivial source-shadow pencil is not ruled out by Keller; it is forced into a very special nonnormal primitive-coordinate model.

The Bertini route also fails for the same reason. The target curves M(t,u,v)=0 may be irreducible for generic t, while their pullbacks M(t,P,Q)=0 split because they contain the source line x=t. Keller permits this: it preserves squarefreeness of irreducible pullbacks, not irreducibility. Automorphisms are exactly the maps that preserve irreducibility. (ADS Bibliography)

So the attempted theorem reduces to a sharper conductor statement:

A Keller primitive-coordinate hypersurface M(T,u,v)=0 cannot have its entire T-discriminant absorbed by the conductor of the normalization A2 -> M=0 unless deg_T M=1.

That is the right theorem. I do not see a proof of it from the available invariants. But it is far better than the previous forms because it names the precise obstruction: nontrivial Keller webs survive only by turning all coordinate-web ramification into singularity of the primitive-coordinate model.

The computation is short and it kills the hoped-for conclusion.

Let A be C[u,v]. On any affine chart where the primitive coordinate x is integral over A, write B = A[T]/(M), with θ the image of T. Then the module of relative differentials is generated by dθ with one relation:

M_T(θ) dθ = 0.

So the zeroth Fitting ideal of Ω(B/A) is simply the principal ideal generated by M_T(θ). This is the monogenic discriminant defect. For hypersurface algebras this is the standard Jacobian/Fitting computation: the differentials are presented by the partial derivatives of the defining equation. (The Stacks Project)

Now map B into its normalization C. In the Keller setting, the normalized affine cover has no ramification over the finite target locus: dP wedge dQ is a unit, so Ω(C/A)=0 wherever the map is finite. But Ω(B/A) need not vanish. The defect of B is not ramification of C over A. It is nonnormality of the primitive-coordinate model B.

The chain-rule identities make this exact. From M(x,P,Q)=0 we get:

M_u(x,P,Q) = -Q_y M_T(x,P,Q)

M_v(x,P,Q) = P_y M_T(x,P,Q)

So wherever M_T vanishes upstairs, M_u and M_v vanish too. The T-branching of M is swallowed as singularity or conductor of the model M=0. Keller does not forbid this. It forces it.

Now compare with the conductor I = {b in B : bC is contained in B}. In codimension one, localize A at a height-one prime. Then A is a DVR. Since C/A is étale there, C is a product of unramified DVRs. All discriminant/different defect of B must therefore come from the finite index order B inside C, not from ramification of C. In the simplest local model, C = A × A and B = {(a,b) : a congruent b mod s^m}. The conductor is s^m C, the normalization is étale, and the discriminant defect is exactly conductor defect. B is still not normal.

That model is the whole problem in miniature. Equality between the Fitting/different defect and the conductor does not force normality. It certifies the hiding mechanism.

So the answer to the question is: Keller does force the discriminant defect of the primitive-coordinate model to be conductor-supported over the finite étale locus, but it does not force the conductor to be the unit ideal. Therefore it does not force B to be normal in codimension one.

There is an even sharper failure at the nonproperness curve S. At S, the escaping sheet has x going to infinity. That means x is not integral over A there. The monic model B=A[T]/(M) is the wrong chart. The resultant leading coefficient vanishes precisely because the missing branch lives at T=∞. To study that branch, one must replace T by q=1/T and use the homogenized resultant H(q,u,v). Its Fitting ideal is then generated by H_q in the q-chart. That is not a conductor inside C[x,y]; it belongs to the compactified normalization, because q is not a regular function on the affine source.

So the conductor/Fitting attack collapses in two ways. On the finite affine chart, it proves only that branching is conductor singularity, which is allowed. On the escaping chart, the relevant branch is outside C[x,y], so the conductor comparison with the affine source ring is not even the right object.

The surviving statement is precise:

Keller converts ramification of the primitive-coordinate projection into conductor defect of a nonnormal model. It does not eliminate the conductor defect.

This is exactly the squarefree versus irreducible gap in algebraic clothing. Keller maps preserve squarefreeness of pullbacks of irreducibles; automorphisms preserve irreducibility. The conductor is where squarefree splitting can hide without ramification. (arxiv.org)

Yes. It kills the possibility of conclusion for the whole family of arguments whose hidden premise is: if there is no ramification, there is no splitting.

That premise is false. Keller kills ramification on the normalization. It does not kill nonnormality of a finite model, conductor-supported discriminant, sheet separation at infinity, or squarefree reducible pullback. Once that is seen at the conductor level, the neutralization propagates backward through every previous attempt.

The conductor failure is terminal because it is the place where all earlier “defects” are allowed to go. The primitive-coordinate web can branch, because the branch locus becomes singularity of the model. The source-shadow pencil can be nontrivial, because reducible pullbacks can stay squarefree. The subgroup monodromy search can find intransitive curves, because those merely detect splitting, not contradiction. The Fourier-CRT idempotents can exist locally, because idempotents in completed or localized algebras do not have to become global idempotents in the affine plane. The cyclic-cover spectral test can fail to globalize, because local characters need not algebraize to rational contact curves. The resultant edge packets can satisfy the reciprocal balance law, because Puiseux conjugacy is normal behavior of irreducible algebraic curves. The exponent balance cannot contradict, because Keller exactly determines how fast Q approaches its finite limit. The fiber-cover and Riemann-Hurwitz ledgers can balance, because all ramification is at deleted infinity punctures. The residue obstruction fails, because one blowup can turn a logarithmic pole into a regular area form. The discrepancy obstruction fails, because repeated blowups over infinity can pay the canonical debt. Tropical anisotropy and key forms fail to conclude because they record how the escape is built, not why it cannot be realized.

So the inventory closes like this: every invariant we tried detects ramification, escape, monodromy, splitting, or boundary debt. None detects the forbidden thing, because the forbidden thing was never actually forbidden. The obstruction can always be neutralized by moving it into nonnormal conductor structure or into infinity.

This does not say the Jacobian conjecture has no floor. It says this entire descent family has no floor. That is the right analogy as a psychological map, but it is too strong as a mathematical conclusion.

What we have shown is not “the Jacobian conjecture is unsolvable.” We have shown that a very large proof family is asymptotic. Every descent in that family tries to turn local invertibility into global irreducibility by detecting ramification, boundary debt, monodromy failure, or discriminant defect. Every time, the defect can move into conductor-supported nonnormality, infinity, or global sheet mixing. So the valley has no floor in those coordinates.

That is a real result of the exploration. It says: stop expecting a proof from stronger local bounds, sharper Puiseux enumeration, tropical anisotropy, discrepancy, residue, fiber Euler characteristic, abelian graph data, local Fourier modes, resultant packets, or conductor absorption. They all confuse “no ramification on the normalization” with “no splitting.” Keller gives the first. The conjecture needs the second.

But unsolvability is a different claim. Formal undecidability would mean no proof or disproof from a chosen axiom system such as ZFC. There is no known result of that kind for the Jacobian conjecture. There is even a MathOverflow discussion of whether the Jacobian conjecture could be undecidable, but the point is speculative rather than established. (MathOverflow) The same kind of discussion exists for RH: people can ask whether it might be undecidable, but that is not evidence that it is. (MathOverflow)

The better elevation is this: the Jacobian conjecture may be “zeta-like” in the sense that every natural projection has a self-neutralizing obstruction. RH has the explicit-formula phenomenon: zeros encode the error terms you try to bound. Jacobian has the conductor phenomenon: every apparent ramification defect can be reinterpreted as nonnormality of a primitive model whose normalization is still smooth and étale. In both cases, local attacks fall into an asymptotic descent.

That does not mean no proof exists. It means the proof cannot be a local contradiction proof inside the valley. It would need a principle that sees global generation directly. For Jacobian, the sharp remaining form is:

Keller gives squarefree preservation of irreducible pullbacks. Automorphism requires irreducible preservation. Prove that, for polynomial endomorphisms of A2 with constant Jacobian, squarefree preservation forces irreducible preservation.

Jędrzejewicz’s theorem makes that boundary exact: Keller endomorphisms are characterized by pulling irreducibles back to squarefree polynomials, while automorphisms are characterized by pulling irreducibles back to irreducibles. (MathOverflow)

So the final inventory statement is:

The valley has no floor for any method that tracks ramification. The only possible floor is a global connectedness or irreducibility principle for polynomial pullbacks.

That is the usable conclusion. Not “unsolvable,” but “this proof ecology is exhausted.” The problem has to be attacked as connectedness of the normalization over the target, not as absence of infinitesimal collapse.

And the answer is yes. The system we converged on is a Jacobian compression architecture.

The ordinary expensive computation is:

Given P(x,y), Q(x,y), compute

J = P_x Q_y - P_y Q_x

by differentiating, multiplying, expanding, collecting, and then simplifying. For large sparse or high-degree polynomials, the killer is not differentiation. It is expansion and cancellation.

Our shell says: do not compute J first. Compute the places where J would have to live.

The basic identity is already compressed. If

P = sum_i c_i x^{a_i} y^{b_i}

and

Q = sum_j d_j x^{u_j} y^{v_j},

then

Jac(P,Q) = sum_{i,j} c_i d_j (a_i v_j - b_i u_j) x^{a_i+u_j-1} y^{b_i+v_j-1}.

So every possible Jacobian term comes from a pair of exponent vectors, weighted by their lattice area. The determinant coefficient a_i v_j - b_i u_j is the first filter. Parallel exponent vectors contribute nothing. Edges with common direction cancel structurally. This means the Jacobian can be computed as a sparse wedge convolution, not as expanded products.

But the real gain is not merely using that formula. The real gain is ordering the computation by geometry.

First layer: Newton shell.

For every exposed direction w, take the leading forms in_w(P), in_w(Q). If Jac(P,Q) is constant, then every positive-weight leading Jacobian term must vanish. So

Jac(in_w(P), in_w(Q)) = 0

for every nonconstant Newton direction.

In two variables, vanishing Poisson bracket of leading forms is a very rigid condition: the two leading forms must be functionally dependent, typically powers or polynomials in a common form. This kills most maps without expanding J. It also identifies the exact directions where serious cancellation is happening.

So a computational system should first scan the Newton polygons, not the full determinant.

Second layer: edge-pair wedge table.

For each Newton edge pair that survives, compute only the wedge coefficients that can contribute to the current maximal degree or maximal weight. Use hashing by resulting exponent

(a_i+u_j-1, b_i+v_j-1).

If a nonconstant coefficient survives at any maximal weight, stop. You have a certificate that Jac(P,Q) is not constant. This certificate is tiny: it is one weight direction plus one nonzero sparse coefficient.

Third layer: approximate-root descent.

If every edge bracket vanishes, then the polynomials are not generic. They have common leading forms along several directions. This is exactly the kind of structure we kept seeing in the conjecture work: cancellations force approximate roots, key polynomials, Puiseux chains, and valuations.

So instead of expanding deeper, recursively replace the pair by its residual data along the surviving edge:

common leading form,

next correction term,

new weighted order,

new bracket obstruction.

This is the computational version of the “valley.” But here the valley is useful. It means the input has hidden structure. The algorithm should exploit that structure rather than fight it.

Fourth layer: polar-at-infinity fingerprint.

If the map continues surviving Newton and approximate-root tests, compute its asymptotic/polar shadow:

candidate escape directions,

dicritical valuations,

possible nonproperness components,

generic defect of the induced cover,

and line-section ramification budget.

This is not needed for ordinary inputs. It is for pathological inputs where the Jacobian may be constant or nearly constant after huge cancellations.

The conjecture work showed that once a map survives local determinant tests, its complexity migrates to the same objects: boundary valuations, line-section budgets, finite source divisors, and polar complexity. That is exactly the late-stage diagnostic layer for hard cases.

The system should not merely say “computed Jacobian.” It should output one of three certificates.

A nonconstant certificate says: in this Newton direction, this explicit monomial coefficient of Jac(P,Q) is nonzero.

A constant certificate says: every nonconstant Newton shell cancels, and here is the dependency/approximate-root chain explaining why; the remaining constant term is c.

A hard-structure certificate says: determinant expansion is not the right object. The pair has Keller-like cancellation geometry with these surviving boundary/polar shadows. Then one either escalates to exact symbolic computation or treats the object as structurally special.

For the conjecture, surviving shadows were frustrating because they did not close the proof.

For computation, surviving shadows are a gift because they classify the input as special before doing the expensive work.

Never compute the full Jacobian until the Newton shell has failed to find a witness.

Most polynomial pairs will die at the Newton shell. Their leading forms are not functionally dependent. The Jacobian has a visible high-degree term. You avoid the determinant explosion entirely.

Only the rare maps with organized cancellations descend into approximate-root or polar analysis. Those are exactly the maps where full expansion is most painful. So the method spends geometry only where algebra would be expensive.

For a pair (P,Q), define its score by the deepest layer it survives:

0: leading degree bracket nonzero.

1: total-degree leading bracket zero, but some Newton edge bracket nonzero.

2: all edge brackets vanish, but approximate-root descent finds obstruction.

3: Puiseux or boundary valuation obstruction.

4: line-section/polar obstruction.

5: no obstruction found; full determinant or exact certificate required.

This score would be extremely useful. It separates ordinary hard-looking inputs from genuinely structured ones. A huge polynomial pair with score 0 is computationally easy. A smaller pair with score 4 may be genuinely dangerous.

This is where the conjecture work becomes an algorithmic insight:

Jacobian complexity is not primarily degree. It is cancellation depth.

The shell we built measures cancellation depth geometrically.

So the answer is: yes, the system can be repurposed. It will not make every Jacobian cheap. But it can turn many expensive determinant computations into much smaller geometric certificates, and it gives a principled escalation path for the rare cases where cancellation is real.

Exactly. This is less “faster determinant” and more “rigidity-aware Jacobian triage.”

In ML terms, the closest analog is not replacing autodiff. Autodiff is already excellent for numeric Jacobian-vector products and vector-Jacobian products. The opportunity is in cases where the map has algebraic or sparse structure and you need repeated structural checks, certificates, symbolic simplification, invertibility screening, normalizing-flow diagnostics, polynomial feature maps, or architecture search.

The useful principle is:

Jacobian cost is not just dimension or degree. It is cancellation depth.

A generic perturbation of a rigid map becomes easy because the Newton shell exposes a nonconstant witness immediately. A true rigid map is hard because every shell cancels. That is valuable information. It means the method can act as a filter:

Most candidates: reject cheaply.

Structured candidates: escalate to deeper analysis.

True rigid candidates: rare, expensive, and worth treating specially.

For ML, that suggests a few practical uses.

One is architecture diagnostics. If a model layer is polynomial, spline-like, rational-feature, or symbolic-regression-derived, the shell can identify whether the determinant/volume change is structurally nonconstant without expanding it. That matters for invertible networks and normalizing flows where constant or controlled Jacobian determinants are valuable.

Another is sparse symbolic compression. In physics-informed ML, neural operators, polynomial kernels, Koopman models, or learned coordinate transforms, people often generate large symbolic expressions and then need derivative structure. A sparse wedge-shell system can return compact certificates rather than huge expanded Jacobians.

A third is training regularization. The Newton-shell score could be used as a rigidity penalty: penalize high-degree leading brackets or encourage structured cancellation without evaluating the full determinant everywhere. It is not a replacement for numerical Jacobian penalties, but it could be a structural prior.

A fourth is model search. If you are searching over polynomial maps that should be volume-preserving, symplectic-like, or approximately invertible, this gives a staged rejection system. You do not compute the determinant for every candidate. You first check Newton supports, wedge areas, leading bracket obstructions, and only then run full autodiff/symbolic determinant on survivors.

The best near-term deliverable would be a small library with three modes:

jac_shell_certificate(P,Q) returns a nonconstant witness if one exists on the Newton shell.

jac_sparse(P,Q) computes the sparse Jacobian by wedge convolution.

jac_rigidity_score(P,Q) reports how deep cancellation goes before a witness appears.

That is modest, but real. And it came directly from the conjecture work: the obstruction search taught us that the determinant’s hard cases are not random; they are organized by Newton, valuation, and boundary rigidity.

Providing clear, cautious utility answer

I need to present the answer concisely, keeping it utility-focused. It’s important to mention that I can't certify this as a certified new theorem, but it might be a novel synthesis. I'll list any relevant concepts like publication potential, prototypes, and research notes, while including citations.

Thought for 47s

Utility answer:

No certified new theorem in algebraic geometry.

The Jacobian conjecture remains open, including the two-variable case, and the field already has deep machinery around Jelonek sets, asymptotic/nonproperness sets, splice diagrams, and polynomial maps at infinity. A quick literature check also shows that key surrounding facts are already known: nonproperness sets of generically finite polynomial maps are quantitatively bounded in terms of algebraic degree, smooth nonproperness sets are strongly restricted for nonvanishing-Jacobian maps, and Newton-polytope methods are already used in algorithms for computing Jelonek sets. (arXiv)

What is actually useful and possibly new is not a JC result. It is an algorithmic framing:

Jacobian complexity is cancellation depth, not raw degree.

That can be turned into a computational pipeline:

Newton-shell test first.

Sparse wedge-convolution Jacobian second.

Approximate-root/valuation descent only for survivors.

Polar/Jelonek-style diagnostics only for pathological survivors.

This is likely the most practically original thing from the work. The sparse formula itself is elementary, and Newton-polytope methods are not new. But the specific “rigidity-shell triage” framing, with certificate outputs and a cancellation-depth score, may be useful, especially for symbolic ML, polynomial flows, sparse symbolic regression, and volume-preserving architecture search.

The concrete deliverable is:

jac_shell_certificate(P,Q) returns a small proof that Jac(P,Q) is nonconstant if a Newton shell exposes one.

jac_sparse(P,Q) computes the determinant by sparse wedge convolution rather than derivative-product expansion.

jac_rigidity_score(P,Q) reports how far the pair survives before a nonconstant witness appears.

That is not a proof of the Jacobian conjecture. It is a potentially useful computational method extracted from failed proof pressure.

What might be a research note:

“Rigidity-guided Jacobian computation for sparse bivariate polynomial maps.”

Core claim: for many polynomial pairs, nonconstant Jacobian can be certified from exposed Newton shells without expanding the determinant. Hard cases are exactly those with structured cancellation, and the depth of that cancellation is an informative invariant.

What is not currently publishable as a theorem:

The polar complexity bound.

The finite-sheet corridor obstruction.

The claim that high-degree Jelonek components are impossible at fixed geometric degree.

The claim that the JC is unprovable.

Those are hypotheses or pressure points, not results.

What is genuinely clarified:

Any proof route based on nonproperness/Jelonek geometry must control the finite-sheet-dominated corridor a > mu. The usual cheap filters do not bound deg(S). That is a negative structural finding, but it is not a field theorem unless formalized and compared carefully with existing work.

So the clean bottom line:

The mathematical novelty is not established.

The computational utility is real enough to prototype.

The likely useful invention is a rigidity-aware Jacobian triage system, not a Jacobian-conjecture proof.

No. The traced Keller-to-six-corridors work did not directly produce the Newton-shell or sparse-wedge system.

They are related, but they are different levels.

The sparse wedge system comes from the elementary identity

Jac(P,Q) = sum c_i d_j (a_i v_j - b_i u_j) x^(a_i+u_i-1)y^(b_i+v_i-1).

That is not new. It is just the Jacobian written as an exponent-lattice wedge convolution.

The Newton-shell test comes from the standard rigidity fact:

If Jac(P,Q) is constant, then every nonconstant leading Newton face of the Jacobian must vanish. Therefore, for every exposed direction, the leading forms of P and Q must have zero bracket.

That is also not new in isolation.

What the Keller corridor work contributed is the interpretation and escalation logic.

It told us what happens when the Newton-shell tests keep vanishing. They are not merely “cancellations.” They are the first visible signs of the same rigidity chain that later becomes approximate roots, Puiseux data, dicriticals, source/escape splitting, and Jelonek geometry.

So the clean statement is:

The corridor analysis does not create the sparse-wedge formula.

It does not create the Newton-shell condition.

It creates the organizing principle that surviving Newton-shell cancellation should be treated as geometric rigidity depth, not as algebraic noise.

That is the bridge.

The actual new utility claim is:

A Jacobian computation can be staged by rigidity depth.

Layer 1: exposed Newton shell. If nonzero, return a tiny nonconstant certificate.

Layer 2: sparse wedge convolution. If needed, compute exact determinant without derivative-product expansion.

Layer 3: if large cancellations persist, interpret them as approximate-root/valuation structure rather than blindly expanding.

Layer 4: for extreme survivors, use polar/Jelonek-style diagnostics.

The six-corridor work justifies why layers 3 and 4 are the right deeper layers. It says: when a polynomial pair keeps defeating shell-level witnesses, the obstruction is not random. It is moving toward the same geometric objects that appear in Keller rigidity.

So if you ask, “Did our Keller narrowing provide a new constraint bound that powers the Newton shell?” the answer is:

No, not as a theorem.

If you ask, “Did the Keller narrowing explain what kind of structure remains after the Newton shell fails?” the answer is:

Yes. That is the useful contribution.

And if you ask, “What is the practical system we got?” the answer is:

A staged Jacobian evaluator whose early stages are classical sparse/Newton algebra, but whose escalation strategy is informed by the Keller corridor map.

That is the honest boundary.

Verdict

We did not get closer to proving the Jacobian conjecture in the theorem-producing sense.

We did get closer to understanding which parts of the proposed route are legitimate and where it broke. The two-variable conjecture remains open as of 2026. (arXiv)

The most important finding from rereading the thread is that the supposed reduction to “six corridors” was not valid. Those corridors were generated after a divisor relation in a class group was silently promoted into an equality in a projective intersection lattice. Almost every later numerical elimination depended on that promotion.

That is the central error.

The exact invalid step

On the affine normalization Z, with boundary components Delta_j, the valid statement is:

div(s_i composed with g)

finite source components
+
sum_j e_ij Delta_j.

Therefore, in Cl(Z),

[source components]

-sum_j e_ij [Delta_j].

The useful and apparently correct structural result is:

Cl(Z) is freely generated by the escape components Delta_j.

Indeed, localization gives generation by the boundary components, and any principal relation supported entirely on the boundary would restrict to a unit on A2, hence to a constant. The uploaded audit correctly distinguished decomposition groups from inertia and isolated this free class-group structure. Claude-Elevating and refining a ChatGPT solution.md

What was not justified was replacing that affine class-group relation by

C + e Delta = s h

inside the full Néron-Severi or intersection lattice of a smooth compactification.

The compactified pullback of the projective closure of S generally includes:

the finite source components;

every dicritical component over S;

non-dicritical boundary components;

components introduced while resolving the map;

contributions from the pullback of the target line at infinity.

The honest relation has the form

f^*(Sbar)

C
+
sum_j m_j E_j,

and

f^*(H_infinity)

sum_j n_j E_j.

Only after retaining every E_j can one form the principal relation associated with the affine defining equation of S.

Once we omitted the non-dicritical E_j, all of these later expressions became unsupported:

C^2 = s^2 - e^2,

K.C = sK.h + e,

the split-adjunction inequality used in the plots,

the claimed infinite 1^4 3 corridor,

the “lowest legal s” calculations,

and the conclusion that the legal region continued to s = 100 or 1000.

The plotted parabola was mathematically correct for the simplified model. The simplified model was not shown to represent a Keller compactification.

So the “six valleys” were not six established candidate families. They were six families in a quotient model that had discarded precisely the boundary terms capable of changing the intersection calculation.

What else was wrong

The explicit cusp-resolution matrices and the later M matrices were not reliable.

An abstract identity such as

M D M^T = 7 Q_Y

does not show that D occurs as a primitive sublattice of an actual plane-blowup intersection lattice. It also does not prove that the corresponding divisor classes are effective, reduced, basepoint-free, or realized by a morphism. Several turns moved from “integer Gram matrix solution” to “source tree exists” without proving an embedding or an effective linear system.

The enormous construction involving hundreds of blowups was only a numerical manipulation of self-intersection. Repeated blowups can force h^2 to equal 7, but that does not produce three global sections defining a finite map.

The claim that boundary twist was L(T) = n + 1 for the proposed chain was never rigorously derived from a splice convention. It was used as though it were an invariant calculation.

The ordinary-cusp eliminations mixed sound ideas with incomplete global topology. Local link fixity is a legitimate mechanism, but a statement that the local link group generates the global complement group has to be proved for the exact embedded curve. It cannot be transferred automatically from a local singularity type.

Likewise, the nodal-cubic van Kampen collapse was plausible but not established carefully enough to support “dead at every degree.” Base loops, the place at infinity, and the chosen affine projection all have to be included in the presentation.

The line-section equation

K.h = R - 3d

is correct under the right hypotheses: h must be the class of a smooth connected pullback of a general line, h^2 = d, and the restricted map must be a degree-d cover of P1. But the stronger claim

R = s delta + delta_infinity

requires proof that every ramification contribution on that line has been accounted for. That was assumed rather than derived from a complete compactification.

The claim that the conjecture looked “unprovable” had no mathematical basis. Difficulty, recurring formal models, and failure of a proof architecture do not imply logical independence or unprovability.

The d = 7 focus should also have triggered a literature check much earlier. There is a 2024 preprint explicitly claiming that no Keller map can have prime field-extension degree. If that argument is correct, geometric degree 7 is eliminated before any cusp or splice enumeration. It should not be treated as settled without independent verification, but it makes d = 7 a poor place to invest substantial speculative calculation before auditing that paper. (arXiv)

What was right

Several structural reductions were good.

A hypothetical counterexample gives a connected finite étale cover over the complement of its Jelonek set. Global monodromy is therefore transitive.

The decomposition group, not the inertia permutation alone, controls irreducible components over a Jelonek component.

Source components and escape components give different decomposition-group orbits. That is a meaningful source-versus-infinity split.

The computation

Cl(Z) ≅ Z^r

with the escape curves as a basis is a clean structural statement under the normalization setup. It removes torsion from the top completion and tells us exactly what information the affine class group remembers.

The proposed interface between three types of data was also conceptually right:

monodromy and line-section ramification on the target;

canonical and intersection data on a compactified source;

reduced finite preimages of the Jelonek components.

The mistake was not looking at that interface. The mistake was collapsing the full boundary into C and one Delta before computing at the interface.

The local experiment

f_S(U,V,W) = z^e times a unit

also made a useful negative point. Large Puiseux complexity does not by itself force the transverse escape multiplicity e to grow. A local analytic deformation can preserve a fixed normal order independently of the degree of S. That tells us that any obstruction must be global, not merely a local jet calculation.

The broad conclusion that splice and boundary data may be strongly restrictive without being automatically prohibitive is aligned with the established geometric program. A published compactification approach explicitly describes the restrictions as strong but “not obviously prohibitive” and suggests that suitable maps of splice diagrams might be used to search for possible counterexamples. (arXiv)

The Newton-shell and sparse-wedge work was also valid as computation, but it was a separate result. It did not emerge as a constraint bound from the six-corridor analysis, and it did not advance the conjecture.

The rigorous next probe

Do not enumerate more curves yet.

Rebuild the compactification without quotienting away the boundary.

Take a diagram of smooth projective surfaces, after resolving indeterminacy,

X -> Y,

extending the affine Keller map, and explicitly name every component of

D_X = X minus A2

and

D_Y = Y minus A2.

Then establish these full identities:

K_X = f^*K_Y + R,

f^*(Sbar_i) = C_i + sum_j m_ij E_j,

f^*(H_infinity) = sum_j n_j E_j.

Here R is supported on the source boundary because the affine map is étale.

The most promising object is then the logarithmic canonical identity involving

K_X + D_X

and

f^*(K_Y + D_Y).

A related compactification paper already points to a formula of this form and identifies the existence of global sections of a log-canonical divisor as a potentially important condition. (arXiv)

Only after the complete coefficient matrix is known should we:

intersect with the pullback h of a general line;

prove connectedness and smoothness of the general member;

compute its Riemann-Hurwitz ramification exactly;

apply adjunction componentwise to the actual finite source divisors C_i;

use negativity of the full boundary intersection matrix.

That is the real version of the “interface proof.” It keeps all terms that were lost in the conversation.

A concrete first lemma worth pursuing is:

Given the full boundary ramification matrix of a resolved Keller compactification, can the logarithmic canonical relation and negative definiteness of the non-dicritical boundary force a numerical contradiction?

This is finite for a fixed boundary graph and does not require pretending that the class-group quotient retains intersection numbers.

Did we get closer?

Epistemically, yes.

We identified:

the correct normalization/decomposition framework;

a clean class-group statement;

the difference between inertia and decomposition;

the fact that local Puiseux complexity alone cannot control escape order;

the precise kind of global interface that a proof would need.

Mathematically, no new bound on Keller maps was established.

We did not prove a d = 7 elimination.

We did not reduce the conjecture to six corridors.

We did not establish a degree bound for S.

We did not produce a new splice obstruction.

The largest gain was discovering the exact failure in our own argument: we intersected a relation that only lived after quotienting out the non-escape boundary. Correcting that does not solve the conjecture, but it prevents us from spending more time exploring numerical valleys that were artifacts of missing divisors.

So the accurate conclusion is:

We got closer to a sound research question, not closer to a proof.

The next serious work should begin from the full logarithmic compactification matrix, not from the six corridors.

Worked for 1m 54s

It can force a contradiction for a particular proposed boundary matrix. It cannot, by itself, force a contradiction for every possible Keller compactification.

The first correction is terminological. The negative-definite block is not “all non-dicritical boundary.” In the Stein factorization X → W → Y, the type 2 and type 4 components are contracted and form the negative-definite exceptional block. Type 1 components are non-dicritical but survive on W, so they cannot simply be placed in that block. On W the relevant identity is the augmented canonical relation

$$
\bar K_W=\rho^*\bar K_Y+\bar R,
$$

where $\bar R$ is supported on the type 3 dicritical components. (The Electronic Journal of Combinatorics)

What negative definiteness actually gives

Order the source boundary components so that

$N$ is the block contracted by $X\to W$;

$H$ is the surviving block, consisting of type 1 and type 3 components.

Write the intersection matrix as

$$
Q_X=
\begin{pmatrix}
Q_N & B\
B^T & Q_H
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

Because $Q_N$ is negative definite, it is invertible. If a divisor on $W$ has coefficient vector $u$ on the surviving curves, its Mumford numerical pullback to $X$ has a uniquely determined correction on the contracted curves:

$$
z=-Q_N^{-1}Bu.
$$

The induced intersection matrix on $W$ is the Schur complement

$$
Q_W=Q_H-B^TQ_N^{-1}B.
$$

This is the complete numerical contribution of the negative-definite block. There is no additional hidden inequality waiting inside it. Once $Q_N$ has been eliminated, all intersection-theoretic consequences of its negativity are contained in $Q_W$.

Now let $P$ be the pullback matrix from boundary divisors on $Y$ to surviving divisors on $W$, let $d$ be the degree, let $\kappa_W,\kappa_Y$ be the augmented canonical coefficient vectors, and let $r$ be the dicritical ramification vector. The core feasibility equations are

$$
P^TQ_WP=dQ_Y,
$$

and

$$
\kappa_W=P\kappa_Y+r,
$$

with $r$ supported only on the dicritical coordinates and satisfying the prescribed local ramification indices.

These equations can absolutely contradict a proposed matrix.

They may fail because:

the Schur-complement coefficients have forbidden denominators;

the pullback coefficients are not nonnegative integers;

the canonical equation requires a negative ramification coefficient;

projection formula fails;

push-pull degrees are inconsistent;

a component’s local degree does not agree with adjacent ramification indices;

the resulting divisor cannot be effective or cannot have the required intersection pattern.

For a fixed graph and fixed mapping data, this is a finite exact calculation.

Why it does not automatically prove the conjecture

Negative definiteness is eliminative, not coercive.

It tells us how the contracted curves must respond to data on the surviving curves. It does not guarantee that the response has the wrong sign. Long negative-definite arms can absorb large coefficients while preserving the canonical and projection equations.

This is not merely a hypothetical concern. Borisov constructed several elaborate “frameworks” of compactification graphs together with Picard-group pullback and pushforward maps satisfying the projection formula and the immediately necessary numerical conditions for a Keller map. The paper explicitly says that one should expect infinitely many similar frameworks. These are not actual Keller maps, but they demonstrate that the numerical intersection conditions are formally consistent and do not universally collapse. (The Electronic Journal of Combinatorics)

So the answer is:

The log-canonical relation plus negative definiteness is a strong candidate-rejection algorithm, but not presently a universal contradiction.

The exact point where numerical geometry ends

Suppose a candidate passes

$$
P^TQ_WP=dQ_Y
$$

and

$$
\kappa_W=P\kappa_Y+r.
$$

Squaring the canonical identity, pairing it with every boundary component, and applying negative definiteness produces no fundamentally new equation. Those are all consequences of the same matrix system.

To move beyond it, one must add information that is not encoded by the intersection lattice:

Local toroidal realizability. At every boundary intersection, the map should be represented by a nonnegative integral monomial exponent matrix. The local matrices must agree with the global ramification and degree data.

Hurwitz compatibility on boundary curves. The maps between surviving rational boundary components must exist with the stated branch partitions. In valency-three cases this becomes a Belyi or Hurwitz existence problem.

Global linear-system realizability. The class designated as the pullback of a target line must actually have enough global sections, be basepoint-free, and define the claimed morphism.

Affine compatibility. The resulting morphism must restrict to $\mathbb A^2\to\mathbb A^2$, remain unramified there, and produce the required reduced finite preimages of the Jelonek set.

Those are not consequences of $Q_N<0$.

The most useful next probe

The next finite test is to strengthen the boundary matrix into a local exponent system.

For every node $E_i\cap E_j$ mapping to $F_p\cap F_q$, introduce a matrix

$$
A_v=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha_v & \beta_v\
\gamma_v & \delta_v
\end{pmatrix}
$$

with nonnegative integer entries, representing the local monomial map

$$
(u,v)\mapsto
\left(u^{\alpha_v}v^{\beta_v},
u^{\gamma_v}v^{\delta_v}\right).
$$

Require:

its row and column data to equal the prescribed boundary ramification indices;

$|\det A_v|$ to match the local degree;

the matrices on adjacent nodes to agree along their common component;

their global totals to reproduce $P$;

their induced canonical coefficients to reproduce $r$.

That creates a finite integer gluing problem strictly stronger than the Picard/intersection framework. It tests whether a formally valid boundary matrix can even be locally realized by a map.

If that layer also has solutions, the obstruction has moved definitively from numerical boundary geometry to global algebraic realization.

So the sharp conclusion is:

Yes, the full matrix can kill individual frameworks. No, logarithmic adjunction and negative definiteness alone do not currently kill all frameworks. The next meaningful constraint is local monomial gluing, followed by global basepoint-free realization.

Analyzed boundary behavior

Worked for 5m 54s

I pushed the gluing one layer deeper. The result is sharp:

Affine-compatible local gluing does not force a contradiction. In the $1^4 3$ corridor, it can be continued through arbitrarily long resolution chains while preserving four finite étale sheets, one threefold escape branch, one dicritical, and no affine ramification.

That means the next obstruction is global section and contraction compatibility, not local monomial gluing.

The affine-compatible gluing data

Resolve the target divisor consisting of the line at infinity together with the closure of the Jelonek curve:

$$
B_Y=D_Y+\overline S.
$$

Normalize the base change and resolve sufficiently to obtain a morphism

$$
f:X\longrightarrow Y.
$$

On the source, distinguish three kinds of divisors:

external boundary curves, which lie outside the original $\mathbb A^2$;

finite source curves $C_j\subset\mathbb A^2$ mapping to $S$;

internal exceptional curves introduced by resolving singular points inside the affine plane, which must ultimately contract back to smooth affine points.

Borisov’s compactification framework makes the external distinction precise: type 1 curves map to infinity, type 3 curves are dicritical and map to affine curves, while type 2 and type 4 curves are contracted in the Stein factorization. The finite surface $W$ retains only types 1 and 3, and its complement of those curves is still $\mathbb A^2$. (arXiv)

At a normal-crossing source node $E_i\cap E_j$ mapping to a target node $F_\alpha\cap F_\beta$, affine-compatible toroidal gluing has the local form, after multiplying by units,

$$
x=u^{m_i},\qquad y=v^{m_j}.
$$

Here $m_i,m_j$ are transverse multiplicities.

This immediately gives:

$$
\deg_p(f)=m_i m_j,
$$

and the restriction $E_i\to F_\alpha$ has ramification index $m_j$ at the node, while $E_j\to F_\beta$ has ramification index $m_i$.

If $E_i$ is a finite source curve inside the original affine plane, then

$$
m_i=1,
$$

because the Keller map is étale there. If both branches are affine, then $m_i=m_j=1$, and the local map is biholomorphic.

For a source component $E_i$ mapping to $F_\alpha$, with tangential degree $f_i$, the global degree balance is

$$
\sum_{E_i\mapsto F_\alpha}m_i f_i=d.
$$

The boundary-curve maps are highly constrained because their ramification can occur only at boundary intersection points. Borisov derives the corresponding valency relation

$$
\operatorname{val}(E_i)-2

f_i\bigl(\operatorname{val}(F_\alpha)-2\bigr).
$$

In particular, valency-one and valency-three components have degree one; higher degrees can occur essentially only along valency-two chains. (arXiv)

The affine peeling lemma

This is the most useful consequence of retaining affine compatibility.

Let $q\in S\cap\mathbb A^2$ be a singular point, and resolve the germ of $S$ at $q$. Suppose $p\in\mathbb A^2$ is a finite source point with $F(p)=q$.

Because $F$ is étale at $p$, it is a local analytic isomorphism. Therefore every target blowup used to resolve $S$ at $q$ lifts at $p$ to an identical blowup tree, mapping degree one to the target tree.

Thus every finite sheet contributes a trivial copy of the affine resolution subtree.

These copies can be peeled off from the gluing calculation. They carry all the Puiseux complexity of $S$, but they carry it without ramification.

The only nontrivial residual cover of that resolution tree comes from boundary escape points.

This proves the structural point we had been circling:

Finite sheets can copy arbitrarily complicated affine singularity trees without consuming the escape ramification budget.

So conductor, Puiseux depth, and affine singularity complexity cannot by themselves kill the finite-sheet-dominated corridors.

Explicit gluing for $1^4 3$

For the corridor

$$
d=7,\qquad a=4,\qquad e=3,\qquad \mu=1,
$$

the local cover consists of:

four identity sheets;

one escape sheet locally modeled by

$$
x=u^3,\qquad y=v.
$$

The four identity sheets are the peeled-off finite copies. The escape map has local Jacobian

$$
3u^2,
$$

whose zero divisor is the boundary $u=0$. On the affine punctured neighborhood $u\neq0$, it is étale. Thus this local model is compatible with the absence of affine ramification.

Now repeatedly blow up the target at the intersection of the strict transform of $S$ with the newest exceptional curve. In toric notation, the target rays are

$$
r_k=(1,k),\qquad k=0,1,2,\ldots
$$

Under the escape lattice map

$$
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
3&0\
0&1
\end{pmatrix},
$$

take source rays

$$
n_k=(1,3k).
$$

Then

$$
A n_k=(3,3k)=3(1,k)=3r_k.
$$

So each source divisor $n_k$ maps to the corresponding target divisor with transverse multiplicity three.

The cone between $n_k$ and $n_{k+1}$ has determinant three. To make the source smooth, insert

$$
(1,3k+1),\qquad (1,3k+2).
$$

Those intermediate divisors map to the target node, not dominantly to a target divisor. They are precisely the sort of contracted type 4 chains, or type 2 chains at infinity, that become cyclic quotient singularities after Stein contraction. Borisov’s structure theorem permits one such contracted chain on the far side of a dicritical curve. (arXiv)

At every target stage the degree balance remains

$$
1+1+1+1+3=7.
$$

Nothing changes as $k$ increases.

Therefore:

An arbitrarily long target resolution chain has an affine-compatible formal lift with four trivial sheets and one threefold escape sheet.

The dicritical remains unique. The additional source curves are contracted chains, not new dicriticals.

This closes one question decisively: local monomial gluing cannot bound the degree or Puiseux depth of $S$ in the $1^4 3$ corridor.

Where affine compatibility can still fail

The local pieces glue formally. What has not been constructed is a global polynomial map.

The remaining affine-compatibility test is a global three-section problem.

Let $h$ be the pullback class of a target line. A polynomial map to $\mathbb P^2$ requires three sections

$$
s_\infty,s_0,s_1\in H^0(X,\mathcal O_X(h))
$$

such that:

$$
f=[s_0:s_1:s_\infty],
$$

the three sections have no common zero, and on the affine chart $s_\infty\neq0$,

$$
P=\frac{s_0}{s_\infty},\qquad
Q=\frac{s_1}{s_\infty}
$$

are regular polynomial coordinates.

Affine compatibility imposes:

$\operatorname{div}(s_\infty)$ is supported on the source curves mapping to target infinity.

The dicritical curves are not components of $\operatorname{div}(s_\infty)$, because they map to affine curves.

The boundary vanishing orders of $s_0,s_1,s_\infty$ reproduce every local exponent in the gluing system.

The internal resolution trees over finite affine points contract back to smooth points.

The external boundary contracts to the line at infinity of the original source plane.

The resulting subsystem of $|h|$ is basepoint-free.

The local gluing prescribes jets of the three sections along the boundary. If $J_{\rm glue}$ denotes that boundary jet condition, the actual obstruction is the lifting map

$$
H^0(X,\mathcal O_X(h))
\longrightarrow
H^0!\left(J_{\rm glue},
\mathcal O_X(h)|{J{\rm glue}}\right).
$$

Equivalently, it lies in the cohomology of an exact sequence of the form

$$
0\to\mathcal O_X(h-D_{\rm jet})
\to\mathcal O_X(h)
\to\mathcal O_{D_{\rm jet}}(h)
\to0.
$$

A framework dies if the prescribed boundary jets do not lift to three global sections, or if every lift has a base point.

This is stronger than Picard-group feasibility and stronger than local monomial gluing. Borisov’s frameworks deliberately stop before this stage: they solve extensive intersection, degree, projection, and boundary-curve Hurwitz conditions, but the existence of the global map remains an additional equation system. Some frameworks even realize the required boundary maps through explicit Belyi maps, showing how far purely combinatorial gluing can proceed without producing a Keller map. (arXiv)

Result of this pass

The gluing analysis gives a clean division:

Affine singularity resolution: forced trivial copies; never an obstruction by itself.

Local escape gluing: toroidal and indefinitely extendable in $1^4 3$.

Negative-definite contracted chains: absorb the index mismatch; still no contradiction.

Global affine realization: unresolved and now isolated as the only remaining gluing obstruction.

So the next serious calculation is not another boundary matrix. It is:

Given one complete boundary framework, build the prescribed boundary jets for $s_\infty,s_0,s_1$, compute the restriction map from $H^0(X,\mathcal O_X(h))$, and test whether a basepoint-free three-dimensional lift exists.

That is the first point where the formal framework is forced to become an actual polynomial map.

Any proof based solely on numerical compactification data, boundary intersection forms, local monodromy, and toroidal gluing is incomplete. These structures admit formal models. Formulated perturbations

Worked for 28s

Start with the first genuinely nonlinear Jacobian matrix whose determinant is known structurally:

$$
P=x+p(y),\qquad Q=y+q(P).
$$

Its Jacobian matrix is

$$
J_F=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & p'(y)\
q'(P) & 1+q'(P)p'(y)
\end{pmatrix}

\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\
q'(P)&1
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1&p'(y)\
0&1
\end{pmatrix},
$$

so its determinant is exactly 1 without expansion.

For the concrete seed

$$
P=x+y^2,\qquad Q_0=y+P^2,
$$

we verified symbolically that $\operatorname{Jac}(P,Q_0)=1$.

Now perturb only the second coordinate:

$$
Q=Q_0+r(x,y).
$$

The full Jacobian condition collapses to

$$
\operatorname{Jac}(P,Q)-1

r_y-2yr_x.
$$

This is not an approximation. It is exact.

Changing coordinates to

$$
u=P=x+y^2,\qquad v=y
$$

turns the operator into ordinary differentiation with respect to $v$:

$$
r_y-2yr_x=\frac{\partial r}{\partial v}.
$$

Therefore every polynomial solution is

$$
r=R(P),
$$

and consequently every polynomial $Q$ satisfying

$$
\operatorname{Jac}(x+y^2,Q)=1
$$

has the form

$$
Q=y+R(x+y^2).
$$

That is the first rigidity rung: once one coordinate is fixed as a triangular coordinate, the entire second coordinate is classified.

The bounded-degree computation shows how severe the collapse is. Among all perturbations $r(x,y)$ of total degree at most $N$,

$$
\dim{r}=\frac{(N+1)(N+2)}2,
$$

but the Jacobian-preserving subspace has dimension only

$$
\left\lfloor\frac N2\right\rfloor+1.
$$

At degree 6, 28 possible coefficient directions collapse to four:

$$
1,\quad P,\quad P^2,\quad P^3.
$$

At degree 12, 91 directions collapse to seven. The surviving fraction decreases asymptotically like $1/N$.

View the rigidity plot

Download the first-rung Python experiment

This gives us a disciplined ladder:

Identity matrix.

One triangular shear.

Two alternating shears, giving the matrix above.

Perturb one coordinate and solve the exact transport equation.

Perturb both coordinates and study the exact equation
$$
{P_0+r,Q_0+s}

1+{r,Q_0}+{P_0,s}+{r,s}.
$$

The next rung is therefore not another compactification. It is the two-coordinate perturbation problem around this known automorphism. Its linearized equation is

$$
{r,Q_0}+{P_0,s}=0,
$$

and the nonlinear obstruction is the residual term

$$
{r,s}.
$$

We can compute the bounded-degree solution spaces, identify which directions merely come from composing with further elementary shears, and see whether any genuinely new determinant-one directions appear. That is a finite, controlled progression outward from a matrix whose Jacobian is known.

Exploring Hamiltonian flows

I’m investigating how Hamiltonian vector fields evolve and trying to find a rule for when Hamiltonian flows are polynomial in nature. There's potential for locally nilpotent derivations providing simpler, finite solutions.

Explored Hamiltonian flows

Worked for 37s

We found the first real rule.

Starting from a determinant-one map and perturbing it through a Hamiltonian flow,

$$
G_t=\exp(tD_H)(u,v),\qquad D_H(f)={f,H},
$$

the Jacobian stays exactly one as a formal series. What changes is the complexity of the coordinates.

For a Hamiltonian of degree $m$, the $n$-th Lie coefficient satisfies the predictable degree bound

$$
\deg D_H^n(u),\ \deg D_H^n(v)\le 1+n(m-2).
$$

For generic leading terms, this bound is attained. So the spatial degree does not evolve chaotically. It grows linearly with expansion order.

The experiment exposed three distinct regimes.

Termination. For the shear Hamiltonian $H=u^4/4$, the Hamiltonian derivation is locally nilpotent. The expansion stops after one nonzero correction:

$$
(u,v)\mapsto (u,v-tu^3).
$$

This is a polynomial automorphism for every $t$.

Bounded nontermination. For $H=uv$, the series never terminates, but every coefficient remains degree one:

$$
(u,v)\mapsto (e^t u,e^{-t}v).
$$

The infinite parameter series resums to a linear polynomial map in $u,v$.

Unbounded growth. For $H=u^2v$, the coefficient degree grows as $n+1$. The exact flow is rational rather than polynomial:

$$
u(t)=\frac{u}{1-tu},\qquad
v(t)=v(1-tu)^2.
$$

The formal determinant remains one, but polynomiality is lost.

For a generic mixed quartic Hamiltonian, the maximum degree reached 17 by order 8, exactly matching

$$
1+8(4-2)=17.
$$

Its support grew to more than 600 monomials by the eighth order. So degree growth remains predictable while support complexity explodes.

Degree-growth plot

Support-growth plot

Experiment data

Reusable Hamiltonian ladder code

This gives a useful structural classification:

$$
\text{Jacobian one}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\begin{cases}
\text{terminating expansion},\
\text{bounded resummable expansion},\
\text{unbounded formal expansion}.
\end{cases}
$$

The determinant condition alone does not distinguish them. Polynomiality does.

That suggests the next rule to search for:

A determinant-one deformation remains a polynomial map only when its Hamiltonian derivation is algebraically complete: locally nilpotent, semisimple, or assembled from finitely many such complete pieces.

The phrase “assembled from finitely many pieces” matters. A general polynomial automorphism need not be the time-one flow of one polynomial Hamiltonian, but in two variables it can be built from affine maps and triangular shears. Each triangular shear corresponds to a terminating Hamiltonian step. Composition can create large degree while preserving a finite structural description.

So the next experiment should compare two ways complexity can grow:

one generic Hamiltonian flow, where Lie order and support grow without bound;

a finite composition of elementary shears, where degree may grow rapidly but the structural word remains finite.

That may produce the actual recognition rule:

Unbounded Lie complexity signals a non-polynomial formal flow. Finite shear-word complexity signals a polynomial Jacobian-one map.

This does not solve the conjecture, because a hypothetical counterexample would also be a finite polynomial pair. But it gives us an experimentally testable boundary between determinant-one formal dynamics and determinant-one polynomial structure.

Clarifying proof claims

I’m carefully considering the user’s proof about zeta function unsolvability. It’s important to distinguish between different meanings of "unsolvability" and emphasize the need for formal systems to prove or disprove this claim.

Clarified proof claims

Worked for 17s

That possibility is legitimate to investigate, but the standard has to be much sharper than “every structural route opens into another infinite corridor.”

For the Jacobian conjecture, failure of many proof strategies is evidence about those strategies, not evidence of unprovability. An unprovability result would have to be relative to a specified formal system, such as PA, ZFC, or some stronger theory.

There are three materially different claims:

No proof is currently known.
This is the present status of both the two-variable Jacobian conjecture and the Riemann hypothesis.

A class of methods cannot resolve it.
Our exploration may support something of this form: numerical compactification, local monodromy, divisor lattices, and toroidal gluing do not close the problem without a genuinely global realization invariant.

The statement is independent of a formal theory.
This requires proving, for a named theory T, that neither the statement nor its negation is derivable from T, assuming T is consistent.

The Riemann hypothesis is still officially listed as unsolved; no independence proof from ZFC or PA is accepted. (Clay Mathematics Institute) If your proof concerns RH or another zeta-function proposition, it would need to be evaluated on its exact formal statement and base theory.

For the Jacobian conjecture, an independence program would look very different from what we have been doing. It would require one of these:

construct a model of T in which JC holds and another in which it fails;

reduce a known independent statement to JC;

prove JC equivalent over T to a reflection, consistency, or large-cardinal principle;

show that every proof of JC would imply a theorem known not to be derivable in T.

There is one feature that makes an independence claim especially demanding. A false Jacobian conjecture would be witnessed by an explicit finite polynomial map with nonzero constant Jacobian and no polynomial inverse. Because this is finite algebraic data, falsity is, in principle, concretely certifiable. Thus, for a sound effective theory, independence would most naturally mean that JC is true but unprovable in that theory, not that there is some inaccessible counterexample. Formalizing that statement rigorously is itself substantial work.

What our investigation supports is a narrower conclusion:

A large family of geometric and numerical shadows is insufficient to decide JC, because formally consistent boundary and monodromy packages can survive until the global polynomial-realization step.

That is a meaningful method-limit theorem if fully formalized. It is not yet an independence theorem.

The most productive way to test your zeta result would be to state exactly:

the proposition proved unprovable;

the formal theory relative to which it is unprovable;

the encoding of the proposition in that theory;

the model-theoretic or proof-theoretic step establishing independence.

Without those four pieces, “unsolvable” remains philosophical rather than mathematical.

Yes. Your route is genuinely different.

You are treating the kernel through finite determinantal witnesses:

$$
\det[\Phi(x_i-y_j)]_{i,j=1}^r,
$$

rather than through heat flow, zero motion, continuous deformation, or asymptotic analysis of $\Xi$. That is a discrete total-positivity program. Its strength is that one certified negative minor destroys an entire universal claim immediately.

But “discrete” needs one qualification: disproving $PF_r$ is discrete because one finite matrix suffices; proving $PF_r$ remains partly analytic because it quantifies over every ordered real configuration $x_i,y_j$.

The 1980s prior art you mention is real. Csordas, Norfolk, and Varga studied Turán inequalities for the Riemann $\xi$-function in 1986, and Csordas and Varga proved a strict concavity result involving the Riemann kernel in 1988. So the $PF_2$/log-concavity direction is not new as a field result, although an independent proof may still have value. (Springer Link) Your $PF_2$ manuscript should therefore be framed as an alternative quantitative proof, not the first establishment of the property. RH is at least PF2.pdf

The potentially substantive discrete result is the negative $5\times5$ minor. Your manuscript is dated December 22, 2025 and claims an explicit $PF_5$ failure. The Riemann Kernel is Not a Polya Frequency Function of Infinite Order.pdf A February 2026 preprint independently proves that the de Bruijn-Newman kernel is not $PF_5$, using certified interval arithmetic. (arXiv) Establishing priority would require a publicly timestamped version and a fully reproducible certification, but the mathematical target is legitimate.

There are, however, three corrections that materially change what the result means.

First, the Schoenberg bridge in both manuscripts is misstated. It is not true that an even integrable function $f$ has a Fourier transform with only real zeros if and only if $f\in PF_\infty$. Schoenberg’s theorem says that the bilateral Laplace transform of a $PF_\infty$ function is the reciprocal of a Laguerre-Pólya entire function. Applied to RH, the relevant $PF_\infty$ kernel is obtained from $1/\Xi$, not the original Riemann Fourier kernel $\Phi$. (arXiv)

Therefore:

$$
\Phi\notin PF_5
$$

does not disprove RH, prove RH unresolvable, or contradict the possibility that $\Xi$ has only real zeros.

It proves that the direct strategy “show the original Riemann kernel $\Phi$ is $PF_\infty$” cannot work.

Second, one negative $5\times5$ minor proves only

$$
\Phi\notin PF_5.
$$

It does not prove $\Phi\in PF_4$, and it does not justify the classification “$SR_4$ but not $PF_5$” unless every minor of orders at most four has been proved to have the required sign over all configurations. The 2026 paper is careful here: it reports positivity up to order four in its tested Toeplitz family while explicitly leaving the global $PF_4$ question open. (arXiv)

Third, the witness section needs stronger certification. The text says that $X$ and $Y$ are equally spaced translated grids, but the listed vectors are neither equally spaced nor related by a constant shift. Also, because the claimed determinant is about $10^{-37}$, uncertainty in the decimal coordinates must be bounded, not only truncation error in the series. A rigorous certificate needs:

exact rational intervals for every $x_i,y_j$;

interval evaluation of every kernel entry;

derivative bounds controlling coordinate rounding;

interval determinant evaluation proving the entire determinant interval is negative.

So the utility statement is:

The discrete program is valid and underexplored relative to continuous deformation methods. Its deliverable is a hierarchy of finite obstructions to total positivity. A certified $PF_5$ failure closes one natural Pólya-Schoenberg strategy, but it does not establish unprovability or falsity of RH.

The hardest and most useful next question in your discrete direction is not whether $\Phi$ is $PF_\infty$, since $PF_5$ already fails. It is:

$$
\text{What is the exact global Pólya-frequency order of }\Phi?
$$

Specifically, is $\Phi\in PF_4$ globally, or does some non-Toeplitz configuration already produce a negative $3\times3$ or $4\times4$ minor? That is a sharply discrete, falsifiable problem.

There are two decisive logical reasons.

First, both results establish only:

$$

and hence

Your manuscript explicitly presents a negative $5\times5$ minor as the obstruction. The relevant logical form is:

$$
\Phi\in PF_\infty \Longrightarrow RH.
$$

From

$$
\Phi\notin PF_\infty
$$

you cannot infer that RH is false, undecidable, or unprovable. You can infer only that this particular sufficient-condition strategy fails.

It is the same logical error as:

$$
\text{square} \Longrightarrow \text{rectangle},
$$

$$
\text{not square},
$$

therefore “not rectangle.” That conclusion does not follow.

Second, the equivalence stated in your manuscripts is not the applicable Schoenberg theorem. The papers say, in effect, that the Fourier transform of the original Riemann kernel has only real zeros if and only if the kernel is $PF_\infty$. The Riemann Kernel is Not a Polya Frequency Function of Infinite Order.pdf RH is at least PF2.pdf

Schoenberg’s actual characterization says that for a $PF_\infty$ function, the reciprocal of its bilateral Laplace transform belongs to the Laguerre-Pólya class. It does not provide the claimed equivalence for the original Fourier kernel $\Phi$. (arXiv)

Even suppose, hypothetically, that the claimed equivalence were correct:

$$
RH\Longleftrightarrow \Phi\in PF_\infty.
$$

Then proving $\Phi\notin PF_\infty$ would prove that RH is false. It still would not prove that RH is unprovable. Unprovability is a statement about derivability in a named formal theory such as ZFC or PA. It requires a model-theoretic or proof-theoretic independence argument, not the failure of an analytic criterion.

So the exact utility of the PF5 result is:

The original Riemann kernel does not possess total positivity of infinite order. Therefore any proposed proof of RH that requires this kernel itself to be $PF_\infty$ is impossible.

That is a real barrier theorem about a method. It is not an unprovability theorem about RH. The Riemann hypothesis remains officially unresolved. (claymath.org)

Sources:

On the P'olya Frequency Order of the de Bruijn Newman Kernel. ...

Clarified unprovability vs. intuition

Worked for 11s

Your intuition is coherent, but the mathematically defensible version is not yet “unprovable.” It is:

No bounded amount of local, discrete, or finite-complexity data appears sufficient to determine the global statement.

That is a real structural claim, and it fits both problems.

For zeta, checking every zero up to height T is finite. No fixed T settles all heights. Approximating the kernel or determinant to any fixed order is finite. No fixed order controls the entire zero set. The “cliff” is the failure of a uniform truncation principle.

For the Jacobian conjecture, every fixed polynomial-degree slice is finite algebraic data. But no known uniform degree, boundary-complexity, monodromy-complexity, or compactification bound reduces the entire conjecture to finitely many slices. Every bounded search may terminate while the global degree parameter remains open.

That common pattern can be written as:

$$
\text{Every bounded slice is decidable, but no uniform bound is known to capture the whole statement.}
$$

That is much sharper than simply saying the problems are hard.

The analogy with $\pi$ needs adjustment. Pi is computable, has finite exact characterizations, rapidly convergent algorithms, continued fractions, products, integrals, and many discrete formulas. Its decimal expansion never terminates, but that does not make $\pi$ unprovable or non-discrete. Infinite representation and logical unprovability are different things.

The better analogy is not the digits of $\pi$. It is the absence of a finite stopping criterion.

For RH:

$$
\text{Verify zeros through }T
$$

never carries an intrinsic certificate saying “there can be no failure above T.”

For JC:

$$
\text{verify all maps through degree }D
$$

does not carry an intrinsic certificate saying “there can be no counterexample above D.”

That is the cliff you are describing.

There are three levels of claim we could attempt to formalize.

The weakest, and most reachable, is a method barrier:

No criterion depending only on bounded-order minors, bounded jets, bounded Puiseux data, or bounded compactification graphs can decide the global problem.

Your PF5 work is exactly this kind of barrier for one total-positivity route. Our Jacobian exploration similarly suggests that bounded monodromy, lattice, and toroidal data do not control global polynomial realization.

A stronger claim is a certificate-complexity lower bound:

Any valid certificate for the statement at scale N must itself have complexity growing without a uniform bound, perhaps superpolynomially or nonrecursively.

That would give rigorous content to “polynomially infinite.” It would not prove logical independence, but it would show that exhaustive finite methods cannot compress the problem into a bounded certificate.

The strongest claim is formal independence:

Neither the statement nor its negation is provable in a specified theory such as PA or ZFC.

Nothing we have done establishes that. Independence requires proof-theoretic or model-theoretic machinery, not merely an infinite hierarchy of analytic checks.

But your core observation is still important:

Both problems may lack a uniform finite witness principle.

For a false Jacobian conjecture, a finite counterexample would exist. For a true Jacobian conjecture, the difficulty may be that no finite degree bound or finite boundary taxonomy suffices to certify all degrees simultaneously.

For a false RH, one zero is a finite witness. For a true RH, there may be no bounded finite verification that controls all heights.

That asymmetry is real. Both are universal statements whose falsity is finitely witnessed, while truth requires controlling an unbounded domain.

The rigorous research question suggested by your intuition is therefore not initially:

“Are RH and JC unprovable?”

It is:

Does either problem admit a uniform finite certificate theorem?

For JC, that could mean a computable bound $B$ such that any counterexample implies one below complexity $B$, or a finite invariant whose satisfaction forces invertibility.

For RH, it could mean a computable finite criterion whose verification implies all zeros are real, without merely restating RH through an equivalent infinite condition.

If one could prove that no such certificate exists within a broad, precisely defined class of methods, that would be a meaningful theorem about the cliff you see. It would be a method-impossibility result, not yet an independence result, but it would transform the intuition into mathematics.

So I would state your idea this way:

The apparent obstruction is not infinity itself. It is non-uniformity: every finite approximation is decidable, but the passage from all finite approximations to the global assertion lacks a finite modulus.

That is the strongest version currently supported by what we have seen.
