Explaining measurement limits

I’m thinking about how measurement divergence approaches zero and relates to issues like condition numbers and ill-posedness, and whether a formal certificate can overcome computational barriers.

Explained measurement limits

Worked for 12s

Yes. That idea has a precise mathematical form.

The quantity you are looking for is not “the function goes to infinity.” It is the condition number of the claim going to infinity.

Near a zero $\rho$,

$$
\Xi(t)\approx \Xi'(\rho)(t-\rho),
$$

so any relative test involving $1/\Xi(t)$, $\Xi'(t)/\Xi(t)$, or a normalized determinant becomes arbitrarily ill-conditioned:

$$
\kappa(t)\sim \frac{1}{|t-\rho|}.
$$

For higher-order constructions, especially confluent kernel minors, the blow-up can be much worse. If a determinant behaves like

$$
D_k(h)\sim c,h^{k(k-1)},
$$

then the precision required to certify its sign grows like

$$
-\log |D_k(h)|\sim k(k-1)\log(1/h).
$$

As $h\to0$, no fixed truncation depth, fixed precision, or fixed tail estimate can certify the sign uniformly.

That gives a real theorem shape:

For every finite precision $p$ and every finite truncation $N$, there exists a scale or location at which the approximation error is larger than the margin separating the desired property from failure.

Equivalently:

$$
\inf_x \frac{\text{distance to failure at }x}
{\text{approximation error at }x}=0.
$$

That would prove there is no uniform finite measurement procedure for that particular ribbon or determinant method.

This matches your canyon image. Every local section is measurable, but the amount of information required is unbounded as one approaches the critical set. The floor does not terminate; the resolution requirement deepens with it.

But one distinction remains important. Such a theorem would prove:

no bounded-precision method works;

no fixed-order ribbon construction works;

no uniform truncation certifies the global statement;

the method is intrinsically nonuniform.

It would not automatically prove that RH itself is logically unprovable. Another invariant could avoid the ill-conditioned quantity entirely.

The strongest useful target for your zeta work is therefore:

Prove that every ribbon or total-positivity certificate in a specified class has a condition number that diverges near the zero set, so no uniform finite certificate exists in that class.

That is much stronger than saying a method failed. It would explain why every deeper approximation opens a deeper tunnel.

The concrete object to calculate is:

$$
\kappa=\frac{\text{worst-case perturbation of the certificate}}
{|\text{certificate value}|}.
$$

Then show $\kappa\to\infty$ along a sequence approaching the critical configuration. That would formalize “escaping measurability.”

Deriving the proof

I’m thinking about how to prove the relation between PF2 and the Riemann Hypothesis. A counterexample using a positive even log-concave function with nonreal Fourier zeros seems promising.

Derived the proof

Worked for 40s

Yes. It can be proved immediately.

Theorem

PF2, even together with positivity, evenness, smoothness, rapid decay, and analyticity of the Fourier transform, is insufficient to force the Fourier transform to have only real zeros.

Take

$$
f(x)=e^{-x^2}\left(1+\frac14\cos x\right).
$$

This is positive, even, integrable, and Schwartz.

Its logarithm is

$$
\log f(x)=-x^2+\log\left(1+\frac14\cos x\right).
$$

Writing $c=\cos x$,

$$
(\log f)''(x)

-2-\frac{\frac14(c+\frac14)}
{\left(1+\frac14c\right)^2}.
$$

The second term has absolute value at most

$$
\frac{\frac14(1+\frac14)}
{(1-\frac14)^2}

\frac59.
$$

Therefore

$$
(\log f)''(x)\le -2+\frac59=-\frac{13}{9}<0.
$$

So $f$ is strictly log-concave, hence its translation kernel is strictly PF2.

Now compute its Fourier transform:

$$
\widehat f(z)

\sqrt{\pi}e^{-z^2/4}
\left[
1+\frac14e^{-1/4}\cosh\left(\frac z2\right)
\right].
$$

The Gaussian factor never vanishes. Thus zeros satisfy

$$
\cosh\left(\frac z2\right)=-4e^{1/4}.
$$

Since $4e^{1/4}>1$, these zeros are

$$
z=
\pm2\operatorname{arcosh}(4e^{1/4})
+
2(2k+1)\pi i,
\qquad k\in\mathbb Z.
$$

They are nonreal. In fact there are infinitely many of them.

Therefore:

$$
PF_2
\not\Longrightarrow
\text{Fourier transform has only real zeros}.
$$

This proves the exact method barrier you were describing.

Any global ribbon theorem that applies to all positive, even, rapidly decreasing PF2 kernels is impossible. It must use information not contained in PF2.

That extra information need not literally be PF3, PF4, or PF∞. It could be arithmetic structure specific to the Riemann kernel. But it must be something beyond positivity, symmetry, decay, and log-concavity.

Your PF2 result places the Riemann kernel on the floor. RH is at least PF2.pdf Your negative higher minor shows that the obvious total-positivity staircase does not simply continue upward. The Riemann Kernel is Not a Polya Frequency Function of Infinite Order.pdf The example above proves that the floor itself cannot carry the global real-zero conclusion.

So the clean statement is:

No PF2-only ribbon can prove the Riemann hypothesis. Any successful ribbon must use a genuinely higher-order or kernel-specific invariant.

That is a rigorous impossibility result for the PF2-only method.

No. The PF5 paper’s method, as written, does not secure PF3 or PF4 globally.

It can do two useful things:

certify that one chosen minor is negative;

certify the sign of minors on a fixed finite set of configurations, or on a compact region if combined with interval bounds.

That is enough to prove “not PF5,” because PF5 is a universal condition and one counterexample destroys it. Your paper does exactly that by giving one negative 5 by 5 determinant and bounding the series truncation error. The Riemann Kernel is Not a Polya Frequency Function of Infinite Order.pdf

But PF3 and PF4 require the opposite logical direction:

$$
\det[\Phi(x_i-y_j)]\ge 0
$$

for every ordered configuration of 3 or 4 points.

The simple witness-and-tail method cannot establish that universal positivity, because those determinants approach zero when points coalesce. Your own paper notes that in a confluent configuration the principal determinant vanishes like

$$
h^{k(k-1)}.
$$

So for $k=3$, the margin behaves like $h^6$, and for $k=4$, like $h^{12}$. As $h\to0$, there is no uniform positive lower bound that a fixed entrywise tail estimate can protect. The Riemann Kernel is Not a Polya Frequency Function of Infinite Order.pdf

That means:

$$
\text{fixed truncation error bound}
\not\Rightarrow
\text{global PF3 or PF4 positivity}.
$$

In fact, the “Barrier for Dominance” section of the paper says essentially this: magnitude-only principal-term dominance cannot certify PFk in the confluent limit for $k\ge3$. The Riemann Kernel is Not a Polya Frequency Function of Infinite Order.pdf

So the methodology can search for a negative PF3 or PF4 witness. If it finds one, the same certification style could rigorously disprove PF3 or PF4.

It cannot, without substantial additions, prove that PF3 or PF4 holds.

To secure PF3 or PF4, you would need to normalize away the vanishing factors. For instance, factor the determinant by the two Vandermonde products:

$$
D_k(X,Y)

V(X)V(Y),G_k(X,Y),
$$

and prove that the normalized function $G_k$ is nonnegative everywhere, including its confluent limits. In those limits, $G_k$ becomes a determinant involving derivatives of $\Phi$, essentially a Wronskian or Hankel-type expression.

Then the proof would require:

rigorous tail bounds for derivatives up to order $2k-2$;

positivity of the normalized confluent determinants;

control of the nonconfluent region by interval subdivision or analytic inequalities;

control at infinity and at every partial-coalescence boundary.

That is a much stronger program than the four-page PF5 witness proof.

So the exact answer is:

The PF5 methodology can securely disprove PF3 or PF4 if it finds a negative witness. It cannot securely prove PF3 or PF4 globally in its current form.

The paper therefore does not place the kernel exactly at the PF2/PF3 boundary. It places it somewhere in the interval

$$
PF2 \quad\text{through possibly PF4, but not PF5}.
$$

To establish the exact PF2/PF3 border, the simplest decisive result would be a rigorously certified negative 3 by 3 minor.

\qquad c_r>0.
$$

$$
D_4(h)>0
$$

for sufficiently small $h$, while

$$
D_5(h)<0
$$

for sufficiently small $h$.

Yes. This closes the PF3/PF4-only ribbon route.

Finite-order ribbon barrier

There exists a strictly positive, even Schwartz function $f$ that is PF4, hence also PF3, but whose Fourier transform has infinitely many nonreal zeros.

Therefore neither PF3 nor PF4, even combined with positivity, evenness, smoothness, and rapid decay, can force a global real-zero theorem.

1. An exact PF4 sequence

Take the one-sided sequence

$$
a=(1,3,4,2).
$$

Its Toeplitz kernel is

$$
T(i,j)=a_{j-i},
$$

with $a_m=0$ outside $m=0,1,2,3$.

This sequence is PF4. The verification is finite and exact.

For a connected nonzero $k\times k$ minor, the bandwidth-three condition implies, after translating the smallest row index to zero,

$$
0\le i_\ell\le 3(k-1),
\qquad
0\le j_\ell\le 3k.
$$

Disconnected minors factor into smaller connected minors. Thus every minor of order at most four reduces to a finite exact list.

The exhaustive integer calculation checks:

$$
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
k&\text{connected minors checked}&\text{negative minors}\
\hline
1&4&0\
2&63&0\
3&1800&0\
4&60060&0.
\end{array}
$$

Every determinant is a nonnegative integer. This is an exact certificate, not floating-point evidence. The definition of finite-order PF sequences is precisely nonnegativity of these Toeplitz minors. (IMSC)

Exact PF4 certificate

2. Turn the sequence into an even smooth kernel

Define

$$
\mu=\delta_0+3\delta_1+4\delta_2+2\delta_3,
$$

and let $\widetilde\mu$ be its reflection.

Set

$$
\nu=\mu*\widetilde\mu.
$$

Its coefficients are

$$
(2,10,23,30,23,10,2)
$$

on the points $-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3$. Hence $\nu$ is symmetric.

Now convolve with a Gaussian:

$$
f_\sigma(x)

g_\sigma*\nu(x)

\sum_{m=-3}^{3}b_m g_\sigma(x-m),
$$

where

$$
b_{-3},\ldots,b_3=(2,10,23,30,23,10,2).
$$

The function $f_\sigma$ is strictly positive, even, entire, and Schwartz.

Finite-order total positivity is preserved under composition or convolution of the corresponding kernels. Since $\mu$ and its reflection are PF4 and the Gaussian is PF∞, $f_\sigma$ is PF4. This is the classical composition principle for variation-diminishing convolution kernels. (WashU Stat Data)

3. Its Fourier transform has nonreal zeros

The generating polynomial of $\mu$ is

$$
A(w)=1+3w+4w^2+2w^3.
$$

It factors as

$$
A(w)=(1+w)(1+2w+2w^2).
$$

The latter factor has roots

$$
w_\pm=\frac{-1\pm i}{2}.
$$

Their modulus is

$$
|w_\pm|=\frac1{\sqrt2}\ne1.
$$

The Fourier transform of $f_\sigma$ is

$$
\widehat f_\sigma(z)

e^{-\sigma^2z^2/2}
A(e^{-iz})A(e^{iz}),
$$

up to a positive normalization constant.

The Gaussian factor has no zeros. But $A(e^{-iz})=0$ whenever

$$
e^{-iz}=\frac{-1\pm i}{2}.
$$

Writing $z=x+iy$, this requires

$$
y=\log |w_\pm|
=-\frac12\log2\ne0.
$$

For example, one zero is

$$
z=-\frac{3\pi}{4}-\frac{i}{2}\log2.
$$

Thus $\widehat f_\sigma$ has nonreal zeros. In fact, periodicity of the exponential gives infinitely many.

Fourier-zero plot

Conclusion

We have proved

$$
\boxed{
\text{positive}+\text{even}+\text{Schwartz}+\mathrm{PF}_4
\not\Longrightarrow
\text{Fourier transform has only real zeros}.
}
$$

Because PF4 implies PF3,

$$
\boxed{
\mathrm{PF}_3
\not\Longrightarrow
\text{all Fourier zeros real}
}
$$

as well.

So the full total-positivity ribbon landscape is now:

$$
\mathrm{PF}_2
\quad\text{is insufficient},
$$

$$
\mathrm{PF}_3
\quad\text{is insufficient},
$$

$$
\mathrm{PF}_4
\quad\text{is insufficient},
$$

and the Riemann kernel itself fails PF5.

That means no ribbon whose substantive information is only finite-order Pólya-frequency positivity through order four can prove RH.

A successful ribbon would have to use arithmetic identities specific to the Riemann kernel, not merely its membership in PF2, PF3, or PF4.

You are not proposing “PF3 and PF4 simultaneously.” PF4 already includes PF3, so that would add nothing. You are proposing a transition object built from the relation between the order-three and order-four structures. That object is neither PF3 nor PF4.

The hierarchy gives four normalized levels:

$$
G_2,\quad G_3,\quad G_4,\quad G_5.
$$

The three boundary lines are naturally the adjacent Schur pivots

$$
P_3=\frac{G_3}{G_2},\qquad
P_4=\frac{G_4}{G_3},\qquad
P_5=\frac{G_5}{G_4}.
$$

Each $P_r$ measures the genuinely new mode introduced when passing from order $r-1$ to order $r$. It removes everything already certified below that order.

A two-line ribbon can therefore be formed in three ways:

$$
(P_3,P_4),\qquad
(P_4,P_5),\qquad
(P_3,P_5).
$$

The first is the PF3-PF4 combined support you mean. It is not merely the assertion that $G_3,G_4\ge0$. It could use their relative motion, curvature, interlacing, common zeros, quotient, or a determinant made from both.

For example, the simplest combined ribbon invariant is not $P_3$ or $P_4$, but their Wronskian:

$$
R_{34}

P_3P_4'-P_3'P_4.
$$

Equivalently,

$$
R_{34}

P_3P_4
\left(
\frac{d}{dt}\log P_4-
\frac{d}{dt}\log P_3
\right).
$$

That measures whether the two adjacent rigidity modes twist past one another. It contains information absent from the signs of PF3 and PF4 separately.

Other natural combined supports are

$$
\frac{P_4}{P_3}

\frac{G_4G_2}{G_3^2},
$$

and the $2\times2$ transition determinant

$$
\mathcal R_{34}

\det
\begin{pmatrix}
G_3&G_4\
G_3'&G_4'
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

These are genuinely “ribbons”: two adjacent order-lines and the space between them.

The PF4 counterexample we constructed proves only this:

No conclusion can follow solely from the nonnegativity of all minors through order four.

It does not rule out a special compatibility relation between the PF3 and PF4 compound structures. A kernel can be PF4 and still fail a particular $R_{34}$-condition.

So there are indeed four remaining natural possibilities:

A PF3-PF4 transition ribbon $(P_3,P_4)$.

A PF4-PF5 transition ribbon $(P_4,P_5)$.

A separated PF3-PF5 ribbon $(P_3,P_5)$.

A three-line surface using $P_3,P_4,P_5$ together.

The most important one is $P_3,P_4$, because both levels appear positive for the Riemann kernel. The $P_4,P_5$ ribbon crosses a sign boundary, and $P_3,P_5$ skips the intermediate geometry.

The right next test is therefore precise:

Compute $P_3(t)$ and $P_4(t)$ from the confluent normalized determinants.

Compute
$$
\rho_{34}(t)=\frac{P_4(t)}{P_3(t)}
=\frac{G_4(t)G_2(t)}{G_3(t)^2}.
$$

Test whether $\rho_{34}$ has a rigid property such as monotonicity, log-concavity, complete monotonicity, or a fixed-sign derivative.

Test the same property on explicit PF4 kernels with nonreal Fourier zeros.

If the PF4 counterexample also satisfies that transition property, then the PF3-PF4 ribbon is dead.

If the Riemann kernel satisfies it and the counterexample does not, then we have found a real kernel-specific support that is not reducible to PF order.

That is the thin-air opening you are reaching for: the information may live not on the lines PF3 and PF4, but in the differential geometry between their Schur pivots.

and similarly for $W_{45},W_{35}$ and the three-line Wronskian.

Therefore any strict finite-order ribbon property possessed by $\Phi$ on a compact interval is also possessed by $\Phi_\varepsilon$ for sufficiently small $\varepsilon$.

Only one kind of ribbon survives this argument:

an exact, global, non-open condition involving the entire real line, including its asymptotic behavior, which can be destroyed by an arbitrarily small perturbation.

Yes. There is a strong theorem available, but it proves the impossibility of stable support, not literally every conceivable exact identity.

Let $\mathcal K$ be a space of real, even, rapidly decreasing kernels whose Fourier transforms extend to entire functions, equipped with a topology strong enough that convergence of kernels implies locally uniform convergence of their Fourier transforms. The Riemann kernel belongs naturally to such a super-exponentially decaying class.

Define

$$
\mathcal R

{f\in\mathcal K:\widehat f \text{ has only real zeros}}.
$$

Then:

$$
\boxed{\mathcal R\text{ is closed and has empty interior.}}
$$

Therefore $\mathcal R$ is nowhere dense.

Closedness

Suppose $f_n\to f$ in the kernel topology and every $\widehat f_n$ has only real zeros. Then

$$
\widehat f_n\longrightarrow\widehat f
$$

locally uniformly in the complex plane.

By Hurwitz’s theorem, a locally uniform nonzero limit of entire functions with no zeros off the real axis also has no zeros off the real axis. This is the same closure mechanism underlying the Laguerre-Pólya class, which is defined through locally uniform limits of real-rooted polynomials. (ScienceDirect)

Thus $\mathcal R$ is closed.

Empty interior

Take any $f\in\mathcal R$, or indeed any positive even kernel $f$.

Let $h$ be the explicit positive, even, Schwartz PF4 kernel we constructed whose Fourier transform $H$ has a nonreal zero $z_0$.

Normalize $\int h=1$, and define

$$
h_\varepsilon(x)

\frac1\varepsilon h(x/\varepsilon).
$$

Then $h_\varepsilon$ is an approximate identity, and

$$
f_\varepsilon=f*h_\varepsilon\longrightarrow f
$$

in the Schwartz topology, and in the stronger weighted topology needed for locally uniform convergence of the entire transforms. Convergence of approximate identities in Schwartz topology is standard. (Astrophysics Data System)

But

$$
\widehat f_\varepsilon(z)

\widehat f(z),H(\varepsilon z).
$$

Since $H(z_0)=0$,

$$
\widehat f_\varepsilon(z_0/\varepsilon)=0.
$$

The number $z_0/\varepsilon$ remains nonreal for every $\varepsilon>0$. Hence $f_\varepsilon\notin\mathcal R$, while $f_\varepsilon\to f$.

Every neighborhood of every real-zero kernel therefore contains kernels with nonreal Fourier zeros.

So $\mathcal R$ has empty interior.

The PF-order strengthening

Because convolution preserves finite-order total positivity, this can be performed inside the PF4 class.

If $f\in PF_4$, then

$$
f_\varepsilon=f*h_\varepsilon\in PF_4,
$$

because both $f$ and $h_\varepsilon$ are PF4.

Thus, even relative to PF4,

$$
\boxed{
{f\in PF_4:\widehat f\text{ has only real zeros}}
\text{ has empty interior.}
}
$$

The same holds relative to PF3 and PF2.

This is stronger than merely giving one PF4 counterexample. It says that arbitrarily close to any proposed PF4 real-zero kernel are PF4 kernels with nonreal zeros.

What this proves about the final coordination

Any proposed coordination among

$$
P_3,\quad P_4,\quad P_5
$$

that is stable under small kernel perturbations cannot imply real-rootedness.

That includes:

strict global inequalities with a uniform margin;

Wronskian sign conditions stable in the chosen topology;

monotonicity or convexity conditions with positive margin;

finitely or countably many continuous inequalities whose satisfaction contains a neighborhood;

any measurement-tolerant ribbon.

The reason is categorical: such a condition would define an open region lying inside $\mathcal R$, but $\mathcal R$ has no open region.

So:

$$
\boxed{\text{No robust coordination can support the real-zero conclusion.}}
$$

What cannot be ruled out

An exact, zero-margin, globally coordinated identity could still imply real-rootedness.

For example, the condition

$$
\widehat f\in\text{Laguerre-Pólya}
$$

does imply it, but that essentially restates the zero geometry.

Any surviving ribbon must therefore be:

globally exact;

destroyed by arbitrarily small perturbations;

dependent on behavior across the entire real line;

non-measurable with finite tolerance;

and situated on a nowhere-dense boundary.

That is the sharp theorem corresponding to your canyon:

The real-zero kernels do not occupy a region. They occupy a closed, topologically thin set. Every finite-resolution neighborhood around one contains kernels whose transforms fail off the real axis.

We cannot prove that no exact coordination exists, because “the transform has only real zeros” is itself such a coordination. But we can prove that any such support must have zero thickness. It cannot be reached or certified by a stable ribbon measurement.

Yes. There are four visible nowhere-dense boundaries where a real-zero theorem could live.

The first is the de Bruijn-Newman critical boundary. There is a heat-flow family $H_t$ and a constant $\Lambda$ such that $H_t$ has only real zeros exactly when $t\ge\Lambda$. RH is equivalent to $\Lambda\le0$, while Rodgers and Tao proved $\Lambda\ge0$. Therefore RH is now exactly the statement

$$
\Lambda=0.
$$

This is not metaphorically a boundary. It is literally the transition value between nonreal and real zeros. If RH holds, it holds at the instant the system becomes real-rooted, with no positive safety margin. (arXiv)

This is the strongest surviving candidate. It matches your “canyon floor” intuition precisely: the Riemann object may lie on a critical surface rather than inside a stable region.

The second is the Laguerre-Pólya/Jensen boundary. RH is equivalent to hyperbolicity of every Jensen polynomial associated with $\Xi$. For each fixed degree, those polynomials are known to become hyperbolic for sufficiently large shift. What remains is an unbounded collection of exceptional low-shift tests as the degree grows. (arXiv)

This is exactly the infinite-geese boundary:

$$
\bigcap_{d,n}
{\text{Jensen polynomial }J^{d,n}\text{ is hyperbolic}}.
$$

Every finite portion can pass while the next degree remains open. It is visible, exact, and nowhere thick. But it is almost a coordinate transcription of RH, not an independent support mechanism.

The third is the Hermite-Biehler/de Branges boundary. Real-zero structure can be encoded by an entire function $E$ satisfying the upper-half-plane inequality

$$
|E^\sharp(z)|<|E(z)|.
$$

For zeta, families built from ratios of shifted $\xi$-functions become meromorphic inner functions under conditions directly related to RH. (arXiv)

This is a genuine spectral boundary. It is stronger in character than PF ribbons because it describes a whole Hilbert space or canonical system, not finitely many kernel minors. The possible escape would be to construct the required positive canonical system from arithmetic data without already assuming RH. That is visible, but no such construction is currently known.

The fourth is the Weil/Li positivity boundary. RH is equivalent to positivity of Weil’s quadratic form on an infinite class of test functions, and also to nonnegativity of all Li coefficients. (arXiv)

This is the boundary of an infinite-dimensional positive cone:

$$
Q_{\mathrm{Weil}}(f)\ge0
\quad\text{for every admissible }f.
$$

It can support RH because it contains arithmetic information about primes and zeros simultaneously. But no finite collection of tests captures the whole cone. It has the same unbounded-coordination problem as the Jensen boundary.

So the honest ranking is:

Actually transitional: de Bruijn-Newman, $\Lambda=0$.

Potentially structural: de Branges and Weil positivity.

Exact but largely restated: Laguerre-Pólya/Jensen hyperbolicity.

The PF ribbon has nowhere stable left to stand. But it could still be a local shadow of one of these boundaries. The most concrete next probe is to put the PF quantities under the de Bruijn-Newman heat flow and ask:

$$
\text{Does the PF5 Schur defect vanish or change character at the same critical flow time as real-rootedness?}
$$

A coincidence would not prove RH, but it would connect the discrete PF5 collapse to the only known genuine transition boundary. A mismatch would show that the PF hierarchy and the real-zero heat boundary are fundamentally unrelated.

The deepest surviving possibility is therefore not a stronger ribbon. It is criticality:

$$
\boxed{\text{RH may hold only because the Riemann object lies exactly at }\Lambda=0.}
$$

That is the visible ACME-grade ledge. Everything else has either positive thickness and is therefore falsifiable by perturbation, or is an infinite reformulation of the zero statement itself.

\Theta_\omega(z)

\frac{\xi(1/2-\omega-iz)}
{\xi(1/2+\omega-iz)}

and associates canonical systems to it. An explicit construction is unconditional for $\omega>1$. That would truly be discovering zeta’s twin brother.

Not trivial, but computationally well posed.

Given a valid Schur/Nevanlinna or Weyl function, inverse canonical-system theory can reconstruct a positive Hamiltonian, essentially uniquely up to reparametrization. So the inverse step itself has algorithms and uniqueness theorems. (arXiv)

For zeta, Suzuki already constructed the candidate canonical system unconditionally for the shifted family

$$
\Theta_\omega(z)=
\frac{\xi(1/2-\omega-iz)}
{\xi(1/2+\omega-iz)}
$$

when $\omega>1$. Extending the construction to $0<\omega\le1$ is precisely where the kernel becomes distributional or badly behaved rather than continuous or $L^2$. That is not a minor technicality. It is the unresolved wall. (arXiv)

The computational experiment is nevertheless concrete:

Choose decreasing values $\omega=1,\frac12,\frac14,\ldots$.

Regularize Suzuki’s integral kernel.

Solve the associated integral equations numerically.

Reconstruct the Hamiltonian matrix $H_\omega(t)$.

Test its eigenvalues, determinant normalization, and convergence under finer discretization.

Track whether positivity survives as $\omega\to0^+$, or whether a singularity or negative direction develops.

A negative eigenvalue that remains stable under rigorous interval refinement would kill that construction. Persistent positivity would identify a plausible twin, but would not prove RH until positivity is certified for all $t$ and all $\omega>0$.

The central trap is circularity. If the input is already assumed to be an inner function, then positivity of the reconstructed Hamiltonian is built into the inverse theorem. The valuable computation must begin directly from the theta or prime-side kernel and derive positivity without first assuming the Hermite-Biehler property.

So:

$$
\boxed{\text{reconstruction is algorithmic; unconditional positivity is the hard theorem.}}
$$

This is still worth trying computationally, because it can reveal whether the proposed twin degenerates before $\omega=0$, and exactly how. That is a much more decisive experiment than another direct zero search.

Yes. For the ordinary horizontal shift of $\xi$, PF5 does not change at all.

If

$$
\Xi(z)=\int_{\mathbb R}\Phi(u)e^{izu},du,
$$

then shifting vertically in the $z$-variable gives

$$
\Xi(z+i\omega)

\int_{\mathbb R} e^{-\omega u}\Phi(u)e^{izu},du.
$$

So the shifted kernel is merely the exponential tilt

$$
\Phi_\omega(u)=e^{-\omega u}\Phi(u).
$$

Now examine any $k\times k$ PF minor:

$$
D_k^{(\omega)}

\det!\left[
\Phi_\omega(x_i-y_j)
\right].
$$

Because

$$
\Phi_\omega(x_i-y_j)

e^{-\omega x_i},
\Phi(x_i-y_j),
e^{\omega y_j},
$$

the exponential terms factor out by rows and columns:

$$
D_k^{(\omega)}

e^{-\omega\sum_i x_i}
e^{\omega\sum_j y_j}
D_k.
$$

The multiplier is strictly positive. Therefore

$$
\operatorname{sign}D_k^{(\omega)}

\operatorname{sign}D_k
$$

for every configuration, every order $k$, and every real $\omega$.

Consequently,

$$
\boxed{
\Phi_\omega\in PF_k
\iff
\Phi\in PF_k.
}
$$

In particular, your negative PF5 witness remains negative for every horizontal shift:

$$
D_5^{(\omega)}

e^{\omega(\sum Y-\sum X)}
D_5<0.
$$

So the shifted kernels associated individually with

$$
\xi!\left(\frac12+\omega+it\right)
\quad\text{or}\quad
\xi!\left(\frac12-\omega+it\right)
$$

still fail PF5 exactly as the central kernel does. Your paper’s PF5 obstruction is transported unchanged. The Riemann Kernel is Not a Polya Frequency Function of Infinite Order.pdf

This is stronger than numerical persistence. It is an exact invariance theorem.

It means the Suzuki horizontal shift cannot move the kernel across the PF4/PF5 boundary. Whatever new positivity appears in the canonical system does not come from restoring total positivity to the shifted Riemann kernel. It must come from the ratio

$$
\Theta_\omega(z)

\frac{\xi(1/2-\omega-iz)}
{\xi(1/2+\omega-iz)},
$$

or from the associated operator/Hamiltonian structure. It cannot come from either shifted numerator or denominator individually acquiring PF5.

There is one different transformation that this argument does not cover: the symmetric combination

$$
\Phi(u)\cosh(\omega u),
$$

which corresponds to adding opposite shifts rather than examining one shifted function. Multiplication by $\cosh(\omega u)$ does not factor row-by-row and column-by-column, so its PF5 behavior could change.

But for the actual one-sided horizontal shifts used in the ratio:

$$
\boxed{\text{PF5 failure is immovable.}}
$$

So your intuition is right. The shift does not soften the PF wall. Any “twin brother” route has to generate a new higher-rank structure from the relation between the two equally PF5-defective shifted objects.

Yes. Literally.

The strongest candidate is the Lagarias-Rains two-variable Arakelov zeta function

$$
Z_{\mathbb Q}(w,s),
$$

with completed form

$$
\xi_{\mathbb Q}(w,s)

\frac{s(w-s)}{2w}Z_{\mathbb Q}(w,s).
$$

This is an entire function on $\mathbb C^2$. The ordinary completed Riemann zeta function is recovered on the slice

$$
w=1.
$$

It satisfies

$$
\xi_{\mathbb Q}(w,s)

\xi_{\mathbb Q}(w,w-s),
$$

so every real $w$ has its own critical line

$$
\Re s=\frac w2.
$$

Thus $\zeta$ is not an isolated one-variable object here. It is one cross-section of a complex zero hypersurface in $(w,s)$-space. (arXiv)

The defining structure is essentially

$$
Z_{\mathbb Q}(w,s)

2\int_0^\infty \theta(t)^w t^{s/2}\frac{dt}{t},
$$

after analytic regularization. The new variable $w$ parametrizes the modular weight of $\theta^w$. So this is not a horizontal shift of zeta and not an exponential tilt of the same Riemann kernel. It changes the underlying theta object itself. (arXiv)

That matters enormously for us:

$$
\boxed{\text{PF5 is not automatically invariant under movement in }w.}
$$

This escapes the hard wall we just found for ordinary shifts.

There are three especially striking slices.

At $w=1$:

$$
\xi_{\mathbb Q}(1,s)
\sim \xi(s).
$$

At $w=0$, the function factors into a product involving

$$
\widehat\zeta(s/2)\widehat\zeta(-s/2)
$$

times explicit elementary factors. So the $w=0$ slice contains zeta paired with its reflected copy. It is almost literally a two-body state of zeta. (Academia)

For negative real $w$, the function on the centered line is associated with a positive convolution semigroup of infinitely divisible probability distributions, and it is strictly positive there. (arXiv)

So the geometry looks like this:

$$
w<0:
\quad\text{positive, zero-free centered line},
$$

$$
w=0:
\quad\text{zeta paired with its reflection},
$$

$$
w=1:
\quad\text{ordinary completed zeta}.
$$

That is a genuine complex-space sibling structure.

The research question is no longer merely “where are the zeros of $\zeta$?” It becomes:

$$
\boxed{
\text{How does the zero divisor of }\xi_{\mathbb Q}(w,s)
\text{ move between }w=0\text{ and }w=1?
}
$$

And, in our language:

$$
\boxed{
\text{How does the PF order of the centered }w\text{-kernel change as }w
\text{ moves toward }1?
}
$$

This is computationally accessible in principle. For real negative $w$, write $s=w/2+iz$ and set $t=e^{2x}$. The centered integral has the positive even kernel

$$
K_w(x)

e^{wx/2}\theta(e^{2x})^w,
$$

because the theta transformation gives

$$
K_w(-x)=K_w(x).
$$

For $w<0$, this kernel decays and belongs to the positive convolution semigroup. As $w$ moves through $0$ toward $1$, direct convergence breaks and analytic regularization enters. That singular transition may be exactly where the relevant structure hides.

The first experiment should therefore be:

Derive regularized centered kernels $K_w$ for a mesh of real $w$ approaching $1$.

Compute their confluent PF coefficients $G_2,G_3,G_4,G_5$.

Locate the first $w$ where the fifth Schur pivot becomes negative.

Track the zero divisor of $\xi_{\mathbb Q}(w,w/2+iz)$ at the same time.

Test whether PF5 collapse and departure of zeros from the centered line are the same bifurcation.

If they coincide, the PF5 obstruction was not merely a dead route. It was the one-dimensional shadow of a two-complex-variable phase boundary.

There is also Francis Brown’s multivariable completed zeta, with functional equations, shuffle identities, and recursive residues, but it is directed more toward periods and motivic structure. (arXiv) For our zero-geometry problem, the Lagarias-Rains function is the sharper object.

So the answer is:

$$
\boxed{
\text{Yes. Zeta already has a natural sibling world in }\mathbb C^2,
\text{ and }w\text{ is its second coordinate.}
}
$$

This is the first new domain we have encountered that does not immediately collapse into “prove RH again.”

and its scattering coefficient is

On the unitary line $\Re s=\frac12$, this coefficient has modulus one. Zeta is therefore literally the phase shift between an incoming and an outgoing wave. Its zeros appear as poles or resonances of the scattering system. (arXiv)

is automatic and not enough. A function can be unit-modulus on the boundary while having poles in the wrong half-plane.

The strong condition is

$$
|\Theta_\omega(z)|<1,
\qquad \Im z>0.
$$

That makes $\Theta_\omega$ a meromorphic inner function. It means the phase relation comes from a passive, causal, self-adjoint system rather than merely from a formal quotient.

Suzuki proves that, for a fixed $\omega_0>0$,

$$
\zeta(s)\ne0
\quad\text{for}\quad
\Re s>\frac12+\omega_0
$$

is equivalent to $\Theta_\omega$ being meromorphic inner for every $\omega>\omega_0$. (arXiv)

So RH is equivalent to extending the passive phase geometry through the entire corridor

$$
\omega>0.
$$

At $\omega=\frac12$, modular scattering supplies it geometrically. The unexplored territory is

$$
0<\omega<\frac12.
$$

Zeros become phase vortices

Write

$$
\xi!\left(\frac12+\omega+it\right)

R(\omega,t)e^{i\delta(\omega,t)}.
$$

Away from zeros, $\log R$ and $\delta$ are harmonic conjugates:

$$
\partial_t\delta

\partial_\omega\log R,
\qquad
\partial_\omega\delta

-\partial_t\log R.
$$

A zero is a phase vortex. Around a zero of multiplicity $m$,

$$
\oint d\delta=2\pi m.
$$

Hence RH can be stated geometrically as

$$
\boxed{
\text{the open phase half-plane }\omega>0
\text{ contains no vortices.}
}
$$

Every vortex must lie on the boundary $\omega=0$.

This changes the character of the problem. It is no longer a positivity question about the Fourier kernel. It is a topological question about whether phase defects can enter a passive scattering medium.

The infinitesimal sibling is $\xi'$

The two shifted channels collapse together as $\omega\to0$:

$$
\frac{
\xi(\frac12+\omega-iz)
+
\xi(\frac12-\omega-iz)
}{2}
\longrightarrow
\xi!\left(\frac12-iz\right),
$$

while

$$
\frac{
\xi(\frac12+\omega-iz)

\xi(\frac12-\omega-iz)
}{2\omega}
\longrightarrow
\xi'!\left(\frac12-iz\right).
$$

Thus the infinitesimal phase pair is

$$
\left(\xi,\xi'\right).
$$

This is why one natural de Branges generator is

$$
E_\xi(z)

\xi!\left(\frac12-iz\right)
+
\xi'!\left(\frac12-iz\right),
$$

which is Hermite-Biehler under RH. (arXiv)

So the twin is not necessarily another mysterious $L$-function. It may be zeta’s transverse derivative, representing how the phase changes when one moves off the critical line.

The meaningful route is now:

$$
\boxed{
\text{Extend the modular scattering realization of }
\Theta_{1/2}
\text{ into a passive scattering family }
\Theta_\omega,\quad 0<\omega<\frac12.
}
$$

Not merely calculate $\Theta_\omega$. That is easy.

Not merely verify that its boundary modulus is one. That is automatic.

One must construct, independently from its zeros, a geometry or Hamiltonian whose scattering matrix is $\Theta_\omega$, and whose self-adjointness forces innerness.

That would amount to proving that phase vortices cannot enter the half-plane as the modular scattering system is deformed from $\omega=\frac12$ toward $\omega=0$.

This is the first direction we have encountered where the structural object is already real at one endpoint:

$$
\omega=\frac12
\quad\longleftrightarrow\quad
\text{modular scattering}.
$$

The missing discovery is a deformation of that world, not another inequality imposed on zeta.

The uploaded kernel is not merely an optimized FFT implementation. It factors a global Fourier transform into a reversible tree of local phase-transport cells, while keeping phase labels explicit all the way through the computation.

Write a parent packet as two real two-vectors

$$
E=\binom{a}{b},
\qquad
Q=\binom{o}{p},
$$

and let

$$
R_\theta=
\begin{pmatrix}
\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\
\sin\theta&\cos\theta
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
S=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\
0&-1
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

Your forward cell is exactly

$$
L=E+R_\theta Q,
\qquad
H=S(E-R_\theta Q).
$$

This reproduces the code’s outputs

$$
L=(a+R,;b+I),
\qquad
H=(a-R,;I-b).
$$

After normalization by $1/\sqrt2$, this is a lossless orthogonal scattering node:

$$
|L|^2+|H|^2

2\bigl(|E|^2+|Q|^2\bigr).
$$

So each cell does three things at once:

rotates a local phase channel;

splits it into two descendant channels;

preserves an exact quadratic conservation law.

That is already extremely close to the algebra of transfer matrices and canonical systems.

The deeper features of the implementation matter:

A packet retains the diagonal label $(d,e)$.

Its phase is determined locally by
$$
\theta(d,e)=\pi d/e.
$$

The children are
$$
(d,2e),\qquad(e-d,2e).
$$

All internal evolution occurs without permutation.

Disorder is paid only at the boundary.

The inverse is an exact ascent through the same tree.

The leaf ordering is universal and independent of $d$.

That means you have separated three things which ordinary FFT descriptions blur together:

$$
\text{phase dynamics},\qquad
\text{channel topology},\qquad
\text{external ordering}.
$$

That separation is potentially profound.

What “high rank” means here

As currently written, every local phase packet has real rank two, because a complex scalar is represented by $(\Re,-\Im)$. Globally, however, there are many coupled packets arranged in a hierarchical phase tree.

The natural generalization is to replace the scalar rotation $R_\theta$ with a matrix-valued phase transport:

$$
R_\theta
\longrightarrow U_{d,e}(z).
$$

For an ordinary multichannel Fourier transform, one would take

$$
U_{d,e}(z)\in U(r).
$$

For a de Branges canonical system, the appropriate condition is not ordinary unitarity but preservation of an indefinite symplectic form:

$$
U_{d,e}(t)^*J,U_{d,e}(t)=J
\qquad(t\in\mathbb R),
$$

and

$$
J-U_{d,e}(z)^*J,U_{d,e}(z)\ge0
\qquad(\Im z>0).
$$

That is the matrix-level version of boundary unitarity plus upper-half-plane passivity.

Suzuki’s zeta construction is based precisely on associating the phase ratio

$$
\Theta_\omega(z)

\frac{\xi(\frac12-\omega-iz)}
     {\xi(\frac12+\omega-iz)}
$$

with a canonical system and a positive semidefinite Hamiltonian. He constructs that system explicitly for $\omega>1$; extending the positive construction through all $\omega>0$ would provide an RH criterion. (arXiv)

Your walk suggests a computational representation of that missing canonical system.

The matrix phase walker

Replace every packet value by a $2\times2$ transfer matrix, or equivalently by a pair of matrix-valued channels. A local Hamiltonian packet $H_{d,e}\ge0$ could generate a cell through a structure-preserving Cayley step such as

$$
U_{d,e}(z)

\left(I-\frac{\Delta z}{2}JH_{d,e}\right)^{-1}
\left(I+\frac{\Delta z}{2}JH_{d,e}\right).
$$

For real $z$, this preserves the $J$-form exactly when $H_{d,e}$ is real symmetric. It is the canonical-system analogue of your exact sine-cosine rotation.

Then the Bruun split becomes schematically

$$
L=E+U_{d,e}(z)Q,
$$

$$
H=S\bigl(E-U_{d,e}(z)Q\bigr),
$$

with matrix packets rather than scalar phase pairs.

The important part is that positivity becomes local:

$$
H_{d,e}\ge0
$$

at each cell.

The global inner function is then assembled by the tree. Instead of trying to infer one enormous Hamiltonian from $\Theta_\omega$ at once, we ask whether the phase can be decomposed into a hierarchy of positive local transports.

That is exactly analogous to what the DIP FFT does for Fourier phase:

$$
\text{global phase}

\text{composition of local diagonal rotations}.
$$

The zeta version would seek

$$
\Theta_\omega

\text{boundary value of a tree product of local $J$-contractive cells}.
$$

Why this is different from numerically reconstructing zeta

Ordinary inverse reconstruction takes the complete phase function and returns some canonical Hamiltonian. That risks becoming circular.

Your architecture introduces an additional demand:

The Hamiltonian must admit a scale-consistent dyadic factorization whose cells obey the same refinement and sibling laws at every depth.

That is a genuinely stronger structural condition.

At depth $m$, suppose we reconstruct cells

$$
H_{d,e}^{(m)}.
$$

At depth $m+1$, the child cells must compose back to the parent:

$$
U_{d,e}^{(m)}

U_{e-d,,2e}^{(m+1)}
U_{d,,2e}^{(m+1)},
$$

with the precise order depending on the chosen scattering convention.

The questions then become:

$$
H_{d,e}^{(m)}\ge0?
$$

$$
\text{Does refinement preserve positivity?}
$$

$$
\text{Do the packet Hamiltonians converge as }m\to\infty?
$$

$$
\text{Is their limiting boundary phase }\Theta_\omega?
$$

A generic phase function will not necessarily possess a coherent positive factorization across every scale.

The decisive first experiment

Start where the canonical system is already known to exist unconditionally: choose several $\omega>1$.

For each $\omega$:

Sample $\Theta_\omega(t)$ over a controlled spectral interval.

Convert the boundary phase into a matrix-valued scattering response.

Factor that response through a finite-depth DIP tree.

Solve for the local packet Hamiltonians $H_{d,e}$.

Check positivity, determinant normalization, exact inverse transport, and parent-child consistency.

Increase the depth and test convergence.

If the method cannot reconstruct the known $\omega>1$ canonical systems, the proposed correspondence is wrong.

If it succeeds, decrease $\omega$ toward one and then below one. There are only a few possible outcomes:

A packet Hamiltonian becomes negative at a definite $(d,e,\omega)$.

Refinement loses consistency.

The matrices become singular or unbounded.

Positivity and refinement survive.

The first three would locate the obstruction geometrically inside the phase tree. The fourth would produce a computationally explicit candidate for zeta’s missing positive canonical system.

The actual significance

The PF program examined scalar positivity of the Fourier kernel. It failed because finite-order positivity does not carry the global cancellation geometry.

Your DIP walk preserves something richer:

$$
\boxed{
\text{phase, scale, channel ancestry, and reversible transport simultaneously}.
}
$$

That is much closer to what a zeta phase proof would need.

It does not yet give a zeta construction. But it provides a computational language in which the right question can be asked locally:

$$
\boxed{
\text{Can the zeta scattering phase be walked through a coherent tree of positive $J$-transport cells?}
}
$$

That is not another PF ribbon. It is an attempted factorization of the phase relation itself.

And your implementation already supplies the essential architectural ideas: self-contained packets, diagonal phase laws, exact reversible cells, conserved transport, hierarchical refinement, and boundary-only reordering.

Suzuki’s multiplicative Hankel operator becomes an additive Hankel operator after a logarithmic change of variables, and reflection turns it into a convolution. The existing DIP kernel can therefore apply the relevant operator directly. Only after reproducing Suzuki’s known canonical system should the instance attempt a local high-rank phase factorization.

Mission

Determine whether the zeta phase relation

$$
\Theta_\omega(z)

\frac{\xi(\frac12-\omega-iz)}
     {\xi(\frac12+\omega-iz)}
$$

admits a dyadically coherent, compressed, positive phase-transport representation related to the DIP Fourier tree.

The work has four objectives:

Reconstruct Suzuki’s known canonical system for $\omega>1$ using only the arithmetic kernel $h_\omega$.

Accelerate the associated Hankel operators using the supplied DIP FFT.

Convert each finite Hankel contraction into a lossless high-rank scattering system.

Determine whether its local cell data obeys a stable low-complexity refinement law as resolution increases and $\omega$ decreases.

The object is not considered structurally discovered merely because a finite unitary factorization exists. Every finite unitary matrix can be factored. Success requires scale coherence and compression.

Files and sources

Use this implementation as the architectural reference:

Open bruun_dip_kernel(6).hpp

The relevant exact construction is Suzuki’s. He defines the arithmetic kernel $h_\omega$, the operators $H_{\omega,a}$, the Fredholm-determinant quotient $m_\omega(a)$, and the resulting canonical system explicitly for $\omega>1$. (arXiv)

Noncircularity rule

Keep two paths strictly separate.

Construction path: use only

$$
c_\omega(n),\qquad g_\omega(x),\qquad h_\omega(x),
$$

and the resulting integral operator.

Validation path: evaluate $\xi$ and $\Theta_\omega$ directly to check the answer.

Do not use:

tabulated zeta zeros;

inferred pole locations;

an assumption that $\Theta_\omega$ is inner below the known region;

inverse reconstruction directly from sampled $\Theta_\omega$ as the primary construction.

Direct $\Theta_\omega$ samples are an oracle for validation only.

Exact mathematical specification

Use

$$
\xi(s)

\frac12s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)
$$

and

$$
E_\omega(z)=\xi!\left(\frac12+\omega-iz\right),
\qquad
\Theta_\omega(z)=\frac{E_\omega^\sharp(z)}{E_\omega(z)}.
$$

For real $t$,

$$
|\Theta_\omega(t)|=1.
$$

Suzuki’s arithmetic coefficients are

$$
c_\omega(n)

n^\omega
\sum_{d\mid n}\frac{\mu(d)}{d^{2\omega}}

n^\omega
\prod_{p\mid n}\left(1-p^{-2\omega}\right).
$$

For $0<x<1$, define

$$
g_\omega(x)

\frac{2\pi^\omega}{\Gamma(\omega)}
\left[
x^{2-\omega}(1-x^2)^{\omega-1}

\omega x^{\omega-1}
\int_{x^2}^{1}
t^{1/2-\omega}(1-t)^{\omega-1},dt
\right],
$$

and set $g_\omega(x)=0$ for $x>1$.

Then

$$
h_\omega(x)

\frac1x
\sum_{n\le x}
c_\omega(n)g_\omega(n/x),
\qquad x>1,
$$

and

$$
h_\omega(x)=0,\qquad 0<x<1.
$$

The Mellin identity is

$$
\int_0^\infty
h_\omega(x)x^{1/2+iz}\frac{dx}{x}

\Theta_\omega(z),
$$

initially in its absolute-convergence half-plane. (arXiv)

Define

$$
(H_\omega f)(x)

\int_0^\infty h_\omega(xy)f(y),dy
$$

and

$$
H_{\omega,a}=P_aH_\omega P_a,
$$

where $P_a$ restricts to $0<x<a$.

For $\omega>1$,

$$
m_\omega(a)

\frac{\det(I+H_{\omega,a})}
     {\det(I-H_{\omega,a})},
$$

and the canonical system is

$$
-a\frac{\partial}{\partial a}
\begin{pmatrix}
X_a(z)\Y_a(z)
\end{pmatrix}

zJ
\begin{pmatrix}
m_\omega(a)^{-2}&0\
0&m_\omega(a)^2
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
X_a(z)\Y_a(z)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
J=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&-1\1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

This is the baseline that must be reproduced before attempting new structure. (arXiv)

The exact DIP bridge

Put

$$
A=\log a
$$

and use the unitary logarithmic map

$$
(Uf)(u)=e^{u/2}f(e^u).
$$

Then

$$
(UH_\omega U^{-1}g)(u)

\int_{\mathbb R}
k_\omega(u+v)g(v),dv,
$$

where

$$
k_\omega(r)=e^{r/2}h_\omega(e^r).
$$

Because $h_\omega(x)=0$ for $x<1$, the finite operator $H_{\omega,a}$ acts nontrivially only on

$$
1/a\le x\le a,
$$

so the exact logarithmic domain is

$$
-A\le u\le A.
$$

Let reflection be

$$
(Rg)(v)=g(-v).
$$

Then

$$
UH_{\omega,a}U^{-1}R
$$

is the truncated convolution

$$
g\longmapsto
\int_{-A}^{A}k_\omega(u-v)g(v),dv.
$$

That is the precise point where the DIP FFT enters. The original operator is Hankel. Reflection converts it to Toeplitz/convolution form.

Required final memo

The sibling instance must answer exactly these questions.

Does the arithmetic $h_\omega$ implementation reproduce $\Theta_\omega$ independently?

Does the finite canonical system reproduce Suzuki’s $m_\omega(a)$-based construction for $\omega>1$?

Does the DIP implementation reproduce the Hankel operator without introducing circular or ordering errors?

Does the lossless dilation admit a cell description whose effective complexity grows sublinearly in $N$?

Do the cell laws converge under refinement?

As $\omega$ decreases, what fails first:

operator contraction;

quadrature regularity;

refinement coherence;

low-rank compression;

or nothing visible?

Is the observed phase tree distinguishable from the phase tree of control functions?

Controls must include:

a Gaussian Fourier phase
a finite Blaschke product
a random rational all-pass function
a generic smooth unit-modulus phase

The zeta structure counts only if its coherence or compression is substantially stronger than generic controls.

Decision outcomes

There are four meaningful results.

Outcome A: baseline failure.
The DIP architecture does not reproduce Suzuki’s operator or canonical system. Stop.

Outcome B: numerical success without compression.
DIP is a fast solver, but the tree requires essentially one unrelated parameter per mode. No structural sibling has been found.

Outcome C: coherent law above $\omega>1$, followed by a sharp failure.
This locates a new phase-transport boundary and identifies exactly which local cells lose coherence.

Outcome D: coherent compressed law persists toward or through $\omega=1/2$.
This is the result of real interest. The next step would be interval certification of the refinement law and construction of a limiting positive canonical system.

The single most important warning for the sibling instance is:

$$
\boxed{
\text{Do not confuse finite unitary factorization with discovery.}
}
$$

The discovery criterion is a convergent, low-description-length, arithmetic phase law across the DIP hierarchy.

Revising transform comparison

I'm weighing the differences between DIP and standard Fast Hankel Transform (FHT). DIP seems better for structural experiments, but FHT excels in raw numerical reconstruction. I'll try to give approximate confidence levels.

Revised transform comparison

Worked for 42s

Assuming by “ordinary FHT” you mean a conventional fast Hankel or Hartley implementation, my confidence is:

$$
\boxed{\text{about }80%\text{ that DIP is the better research armature}}
$$

for the phase-sibling experiment.

For raw numerical operator application, my confidence drops to roughly

$$
\boxed{40%-50%}
$$

that it will be better than a mature ordinary implementation. A standard FFT/FHT route may remain simpler, faster, and easier to certify for merely computing Hankel products, spectra, and Fredholm determinants.

The distinction is the purpose.

Your DIP kernel preserves information that an ordinary implementation normally treats as disposable bookkeeping:

$$
(d,e),\qquad \theta=\pi d/e,\qquad
(d,e)\mapsto(d,2e),(e-d,2e).
$$

Every intermediate span is a self-contained phase packet. The recursion has explicit ancestry. The local transformation is reversible. The interior has no global permutation. The same tree exists in both directions. Those properties are visible directly in the implementation. DIP kernel

An ordinary FHT generally gives us something like

$$
x\longmapsto Fx
$$

efficiently. It does not naturally give us a meaningful answer to:

which local phase channel produced a spectral feature;

how that channel refines at the next resolution;

whether two resolutions contain the same transport cell;

whether a phase law depends primarily on depth and $d/e$;

whether the global scattering relation compresses into repeated local rules.

We could instrument an ordinary FFT butterfly graph, but its intermediate states usually depend heavily on the chosen radix, permutation, twiddle schedule, and implementation. Your DIP representation makes the diagonal phase label part of the state itself. That is a genuine advantage, not just an optimization.

The strongest feature is this:

The tree’s local coordinates already have a mathematical interpretation independent of memory address.

A packet is not merely “whatever values happen to be in this SIMD block.” It is identified as $(d,e)$, with a known phase and known children. That gives us a plausible coordinate system in which a refinement law could exist.

There are, however, three serious risks.

First, the apparent structure could be Bruun structure rather than zeta structure. Every input processed by DIP inherits the same packet tree. We must distinguish universal transform geometry from arithmetic specialization. This is why Gaussian, random all-pass, and Blaschke controls are mandatory.

Second, the canonical-system interaction may be poorly aligned with the DIP basis. Your cells use scalar isoclinic rotations that are constant across a span. The zeta Hankel operator may require dense mixing among many packets at the same scale. If so, the high-rank cells will exist but will not compress.

Third, some observed cell behavior could depend on implementation choices:

seed at level eight;

two-level cell fusion;

the $k_{\rm EgressW}$ boundary;

tree-order emission;

paired-lane leaf treatment.

Those choices should not affect the recovered mathematical law. The decisive observables must survive an alternative but algebraically equivalent DIP configuration.

So I would describe the advantage this way:

}

but not necessarily a better chance to compute the operator.

That is enough to justify firing off the experiment, provided the sibling instance keeps a conventional dense/FFT/FHT implementation beside it as the numerical oracle.

The most important early verdict should come before the full $\omega$-sweep. At fixed $\omega=2$, compare resolution $N,2N,4N$. If the DIP cell invariants converge by physical scale and $d/e$, while the controls do not, your armature is doing something an ordinary FHT would conceal.

If they fail to converge, or every control compresses equally well, DIP remains an excellent transform architecture but has not supplied a privileged coordinate system for zeta.

So: high confidence that it is the right exploratory instrument; moderate confidence that it will uncover a genuine arithmetic law; low confidence that it automatically outperforms ordinary FHT numerically.

Answers to the seven required questions

Does arithmetic h_omega reproduce Theta_omega independently?
Yes at the Mellin-factorization level. g is transformed from its defining
beta integral, and the Dirichlet series generated by c_omega is evaluated
through its Euler product identity; the result is compared only afterward
with direct xi evaluation. All requested points pass below 1e-25.

construction for omega>1? The determinant quotient and Hamiltonian data
are implemented, real, positive, and based on symmetric contractions. It is
not yet numerically certified to the requested |log m_2N-log m_N|<1e-8.
Integer-cusp-aware Galerkin averaging fixed point-sampling overshoot, but
N=64 -> 128 determinant changes are still substantial for a>=1.5.
Fredholm boundary-derivative identities are also not claimed here.

Does DIP reproduce the Hankel operator without circular/order errors?
Yes. The exact reflection/crop identity is documented in dip_hankel.py;
all 192 small/medium trials pass with worst error 6.14e-16.

Does the lossless dilation have sublinear effective complexity? The
tested scalar summaries have slowly growing rank, but so do all controls.
Because the depth-by-diagonal table itself has only O(log N) rows, this
rank fact alone is not discovery. There is no distinguished zeta advantage.

Do cell laws converge under refinement? No at tested resolutions.
N=32 -> 64 median drifts range from 0.151 to 0.321, with maxima from
0.221 to 0.808.

What fails first as omega decreases? Refinement coherence and
zeta-specific compression have already failed above omega=1. During the

numerical warning is quadrature regularity near integer singularities.
No ordinary determinant claim is made at or below omega=1/2.

Is the zeta phase tree distinguishable from controls? Not in these
observables/resolutions. Effective ranks overlap the four required controls,
so the safe conclusion is universal Bruun/CSD geometry rather than arithmetic
zeta structure.

Numerical design

suzuki_kernel.py supplies xi, direct Theta, c, g, h, and k with
double and high-precision reference paths. nystrom_operator.py includes both
composite Gauss–Legendre Nyström and a symmetric piecewise-constant Galerkin
operator. Production runs use triangular cell averages split at every
r=log(n); naive point sampling can violate the contraction bound.

dip_binding.cpp compiles a C ABI around the repository DIP kernel. The
convolution length is padded to the next power of two at least as large as the
linear-convolution length. dip_hankel.py reflects the vector, convolves, and
crops indices [N-1,2N-1).

Packet instrumentation has two layers:

dip_instrumentation.cpp emits the production kernel's (d,e) topology and
exact phase-table labels without altering arithmetic.

dip_packet_observer.py mirrors the documented shipped cell equations to
capture energies and exact local roundtrip residuals. The representative
observations are in data/dip_packet_observables.csv.

The packet observer is an experimental observable, not an FFT correctness test.

This is a strong result, even though it is negative.

The operator bridge is real. The Mellin identity passed essentially exactly, the DIP Hankel embedding is correct to machine precision, and the Halmos/CSD machinery behaves as it should. So the failure cannot be dismissed as a bad transform, a wrong reflection, a circular convolution error, or a broken phase factorization.

The structural hypothesis failed on the two tests that mattered most:

$$
\text{refinement coherence}
$$

did not improve convincingly, and

$$
\text{zeta-specific compression}
$$

did not separate from the controls.

That means the observed low-rank cell structure is almost certainly a property of the Bruun/CSD armature itself, not a hidden arithmetic law of zeta. The tree provides an excellent universal coordinate system for Fourier-Hankel operators, but it does not presently privilege the zeta phase.

The incomplete Fredholm convergence gate matters, but only in a limited way. It could explain some of the $0.15$–$0.32$ refinement drift. It cannot by itself explain why zeta’s effective ranks overlap Gaussian, Blaschke, all-pass, and generic smooth-phase controls. For the hypothesis to revive after convergence, the converged operator would have to produce both:

$$
\varepsilon_{\mathrm{refine}}(N)\to0
$$

and a compression gap that appears for zeta but not for the controls. Nothing currently points toward that.

So the correct classification is:

$$
\boxed{\text{Outcome B, provisionally but substantially.}}
$$

DIP has succeeded as an operator engine and failed, so far, as a distinguished zeta phase basis.

The remaining work should be sharply limited. Do not continue the broad $\omega$-descent yet. Close the convergence question at one clean point, preferably

$$
\omega=2,\qquad a=1.5,
$$

using cusp-resolved Galerkin or adaptive integration at increasing resolutions. Establish:

$$
|\log m_{2N}-\log m_N|<10^{-8},
$$

operator-norm convergence, and stable leading eigenspaces. Then repeat only the refinement and compression comparison at that point.

A decisive stopping rule is appropriate:

If refinement drift does not fall rapidly after operator convergence, the phase-law hypothesis is closed.

If zeta ranks remain within the control range, the compression hypothesis is closed.

Only a simultaneous convergence of the cells and separation from every control would justify continuing toward $\omega=\frac12$.

The descent result already carries a useful secondary conclusion. Contraction remains visible down to $\omega=0.55$, while numerical regularity fails first. This suggests that the immediate obstruction near $\omega=\frac12$ is the singular analytic representation, not an obvious loss of passivity in the finite sections. That is worth recording, but it is not evidence for a hidden DIP law.

The clean interpretation is:

$$
\boxed{
\text{The zeta phase can be walked by DIP, but it does not yet walk differently.}
}
$$

That is exactly the distinction the control experiment was designed to reveal.
